**Guatemala**,[[a]](#cite_note-7) officially the **Republic of Guatemala**,[[b]](#cite_note-8) is a country in northern [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America"). It is bordered to the north and west by [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), to the northeast (in the [adjacency zone](/wiki/Belizean%E2%80%93Guatemalan_territorial_dispute "Belizean–Guatemalan territorial dispute")) by [Belize](/wiki/Belize "Belize"), to the east by [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras"), and to the southeast by [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador"). It is bounded by the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") to the south and the [Gulf of Honduras](/wiki/Gulf_of_Honduras "Gulf of Honduras") to the northeast.

The territory of modern Guatemala hosted the core of the [Maya civilization](/wiki/Maya_civilization "Maya civilization"), which extended across [Mesoamerica](/wiki/Mesoamerica "Mesoamerica"); in the 16th century, most of this was [conquered by the Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_Guatemala "Spanish conquest of Guatemala") and claimed as part of the [viceroyalty](/wiki/Viceroyalty "Viceroyalty") of [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain"). Guatemala attained independence from Spain and Mexico in 1821. From 1823 to 1841, it was part of the [Federal Republic of Central America](/wiki/Federal_Republic_of_Central_America "Federal Republic of Central America"). For the latter half of the 19th century, Guatemala suffered instability and civil strife. From the early 20th century, it was ruled by a series of dictators backed by the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). In 1944, authoritarian leader [Jorge Ubico](/wiki/Jorge_Ubico "Jorge Ubico") was overthrown by a pro-democratic military coup, initiating [a decade-long revolution](/wiki/Guatemalan_Revolution "Guatemalan Revolution") that led to social and economic reforms. In 1954, [a U.S.-backed military coup](/wiki/1954_Guatemalan_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1954 Guatemalan coup d'état") ended the revolution and installed a dictatorship.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlakeley2009[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidrft8AgAAQBAJpgPA92_92]-9)[[8]](#cite_note-10) From 1960 to 1996, Guatemala [endured a bloody civil war](/wiki/Guatemalan_Civil_War "Guatemalan Civil War") fought between the U.S.-backed government and [leftist](/wiki/Leftist "Leftist") rebels, including [genocidal massacres](/wiki/Guatemalan_genocide "Guatemalan genocide") of the Maya population perpetrated by the Guatemalan military.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECooper2008171-11)[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESolano20123–15-12)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENavarro1999-13) The [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") negotiated a peace accord, resulting in economic growth and successive democratic elections.

Guatemala's abundance of biologically significant and unique ecosystems includes many endemic species and contributes to Mesoamerica's designation as a [biodiversity hotspot](/wiki/Biodiversity_hotspot "Biodiversity hotspot").[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConservation_International2007-14) Although rich in export goods, around a quarter of the population (4.6 million) face [food insecurity](/wiki/Food_insecurity "Food insecurity"); other major issues include poverty, crime, corruption, drug trafficking, and civil instability.

With an estimated population of around 17.6 million,[[13]](#cite_note-UN_WPP-15)[[14]](#cite_note-UN_WPP_2022-16) Guatemala is the most populous country in Central America, the fourth most populous country in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") and the 11th most populous country in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"). Its capital and largest city, [Guatemala City](/wiki/Guatemala_City "Guatemala City"), is the most populous city in Central America.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name "Guatemala" comes from the [Nahuatl](/wiki/Nahuatl_language "Nahuatl language") word *Cuauhtēmallān*, or "place of many trees", a derivative of the [K'iche' Mayan](/wiki/K%27iche%27_people "K'iche' people") word for "many trees"[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampbell1997-17)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroika_study_abroad_programs2006-18) or, perhaps more specifically, for the Cuate/Cuatli tree [Eysenhardtia](/wiki/Eysenhardtia "Eysenhardtia"). This name was originally used by the [Mexica](/wiki/Mexica "Mexica") to refer to the [Kaqchikel](/wiki/Kaqchikel_people "Kaqchikel people") city of [Iximche](/wiki/Iximche "Iximche"), but was extended to refer to the whole country during the Spanish colonial period.[[17]](#cite_note-19)[[18]](#cite_note-20)

The official name of the country is the Republic of Guatemala (*República de Guatemala*), since a decree from 21 March 1847, and prior to that it was the State of Guatemala (*Estado de Guatemala*) from 17 April 1839.[[19]](#cite_note-21)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Guatemala](/wiki/History_of_Guatemala "History of Guatemala")

### Pre-Columbian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Pre-Columbian")]

The first evidence of human habitation in Guatemala dates to 12,000 BC. Archaeological evidence, such as [obsidian](/wiki/Obsidian "Obsidian") [arrowheads](/wiki/Arrowhead "Arrowhead") found in various parts of the country, suggests a human presence as early as 18,000 BC.[[20]](#cite_note-22) There is archaeological proof that early Guatemalan settlers were [hunter-gatherers](/wiki/Hunter-gatherers "Hunter-gatherers"). [Maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize") cultivation had been developed by 3500 BC.[[21]](#cite_note-23) Sites dating to 6500 BC have been found in the [Quiché](/wiki/Quich%C3%A9_department "Quiché department") region in the Highlands, and [Sipacate](/wiki/Sipacate "Sipacate") and [Escuintla](/wiki/Escuintla "Escuintla") on the central Pacific coast.

Archaeologists divide the [pre-Columbian](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_era "Pre-Columbian era") history of Mesoamerica into the Preclassic period (3000 BC to 250 AD), the Classic period (250 to 900 AD), and the Postclassic period (900 to 1500 AD).[[22]](#cite_note-24) Until recently, the Preclassic was regarded by researchers as a formative period, in which the peoples typically lived in huts in small villages of farmers, with few permanent buildings. This notion has been challenged since the late 20th century by discoveries of monumental architecture from that period, such as the [Mirador Basin](/wiki/Mirador_Basin "Mirador Basin") cities of [Nakbé](/wiki/Nakb%C3%A9 "Nakbé"), Xulnal, [El Tintal](/wiki/El_Tintal "El Tintal"), Wakná, and [El Mirador](/wiki/El_Mirador "El Mirador").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Tikal_mayan_ruins_2009.jpg/500px-Tikal_mayan_ruins_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tikal_mayan_ruins_2009.jpg)

Maya city of [Tikal](/wiki/Tikal "Tikal")

The Classic period of [Mesoamerican](/wiki/Mesoamerican "Mesoamerican") civilization corresponds to the height of the [Maya civilization](/wiki/Maya_civilization "Maya civilization"). It is represented by countless sites throughout Guatemala, although the largest concentration is in [Petén](/wiki/Pet%C3%A9n_Basin "Petén Basin"). This period is characterized by urbanization, the emergence of independent city-states, and contact with other Mesoamerican cultures.[[23]](#cite_note-25)

This lasted until approximately 900 AD, when the [Classic Maya civilization collapsed](/wiki/Classic_Maya_collapse "Classic Maya collapse").[[24]](#cite_note-Gill_2000-26) The Maya abandoned many of the cities of the central lowlands or died during a drought-induced [famine](/wiki/Famine "Famine").[[24]](#cite_note-Gill_2000-26) The cause of the collapse is debated, but the drought theory is gaining currency, supported by evidence such as lakebeds, ancient pollen, and others.[[24]](#cite_note-Gill_2000-26) A series of prolonged droughts in what is otherwise a seasonal desert is thought to have decimated the Maya, who relied on regular rainfall to support their dense population.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster2000-27)

The Post-Classic period is represented by regional kingdoms, such as the [Itza](/wiki/Itza_people "Itza people"), [Kowoj](/wiki/Kowoj "Kowoj"), [Yalain](/wiki/Yalain "Yalain") and [Kejache](/wiki/Kejache "Kejache") in Petén, and the [Mam](/wiki/Mam_people "Mam people"), [Ki'che'](/wiki/K%27iche%27_people "K'iche' people"), [Kackchiquel](/wiki/Kaqchikel_people "Kaqchikel people"), [Chajoma](/wiki/Chajoma "Chajoma"), [Tz'utujil](/wiki/Tz%27utujil_people "Tz'utujil people"), [Poqomchi'](/wiki/Poqomchi%27_people "Poqomchi' people"), [Q'eqchi'](/wiki/Q%27eqchi%27_people "Q'eqchi' people") and [Ch'orti'](/wiki/Ch%27orti%27_people "Ch'orti' people") peoples in the highlands. Their cities preserved many aspects of Maya culture.

The Maya civilization shares many features with other Mesoamerican civilizations due to the high degree of interaction and [cultural diffusion](/wiki/Cultural_diffusion "Cultural diffusion") that characterized the region. Advances such as writing, [epigraphy](/wiki/Epigraphy "Epigraphy"), and the [calendar](/wiki/Maya_calendar "Maya calendar") did not originate with the Maya; however, their civilization fully developed them. Maya influence can be detected from [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras"), [Belize](/wiki/Belize "Belize"), Guatemala, and Northern [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador") to as far north as central Mexico, more than 1,000 km (620 mi) from the [Maya area](/wiki/Geography_of_Mesoamerica#Maya_Region "Geography of Mesoamerica"). Many outside influences are found in [Maya art](/wiki/Maya_art "Maya art") and architecture, which are thought to have resulted from trade and cultural exchange rather than direct external conquest.

### Spanish era (1519–1821)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Spanish era (1519–1821)")]

See also: [Captaincy General of Guatemala](/wiki/Captaincy_General_of_Guatemala "Captaincy General of Guatemala"), [Spanish conquest of Guatemala](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_Guatemala "Spanish conquest of Guatemala"), and [Spanish conquest of Petén](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_Pet%C3%A9n "Spanish conquest of Petén")

[![Painting of a bearded man in early 16th-century attire including prominent ruff collar, wearing a decorative breastplate, with his right hand resting on his hip and his left hand grasping a cane, or riding crop.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Pedro_de_Alvarado_%28Tom%C3%A1s_Povedano%29.jpg/250px-Pedro_de_Alvarado_%28Tom%C3%A1s_Povedano%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pedro_de_Alvarado_(Tom%C3%A1s_Povedano).jpg)

The *Conquistador* Pedro de Alvarado led the initial Spanish efforts to conquer Guatemala.[[26]](#cite_note-Lovell05p58-28)

The [Spanish conquest of Guatemala](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_Guatemala "Spanish conquest of Guatemala") began in the early 16th century, led by conquistador [Pedro de Alvarado](/wiki/Pedro_de_Alvarado "Pedro de Alvarado"), who was appointed by [Hernán Cortés](/wiki/Hern%C3%A1n_Cort%C3%A9s "Hernán Cortés") to extend Spanish control into [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America"). In 1523–1524, Alvarado launched a military campaign into the [Guatemalan highlands](/wiki/Guatemalan_Highlands "Guatemalan Highlands"), initially allying with the [Kaqchikel Maya](/wiki/Kaqchikel_language "Kaqchikel language") to defeat their rivals, the [K'iche' (Quiché) Maya](/wiki/K%CA%BCiche%CA%BC_people "Kʼicheʼ people"). However, relations with the Kaqchikel soon deteriorated, leading to further conflict and eventual Spanish dominance over the region.[[27]](#cite_note-29)

The Spanish incursion introduced devastating epidemics, including [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox"), which significantly reduced the indigenous population even before full military conquest was achieved.[[28]](#cite_note-30) In 1524, the Spanish established their first capital, Villa de Santiago de Guatemala, near the ruins of [Iximché](/wiki/Iximche "Iximche"), the former [Kaqchikel](/wiki/Kaqchikel_people "Kaqchikel people") capital. This settlement was later relocated to the [Almolonga Valley](/wiki/Ciudad_Vieja "Ciudad Vieja") (present-day [Ciudad Vieja](/wiki/Ciudad_Vieja "Ciudad Vieja")) in 1527 due to indigenous resistance.[[29]](#cite_note-31)

On 11 September 1541, a catastrophic [lahar](/wiki/Lahar "Lahar") from [Volcán de Agua](/wiki/Volc%C3%A1n_de_Agua "Volcán de Agua") destroyed [Ciudad Vieja](/wiki/Ciudad_Vieja "Ciudad Vieja"), prompting the relocation of the capital to the Panchoy Valley, where it was renamed [Santiago de los Caballeros de Guatemala](/wiki/Santiago_de_los_Caballeros_de_Guatemala "Santiago de los Caballeros de Guatemala") (now [Antigua Guatemala](/wiki/Antigua_Guatemala "Antigua Guatemala")). In 1542, the region was formally organized as the [Captaincy General of Guatemala](/wiki/Captaincy_General_of_Guatemala "Captaincy General of Guatemala") (Capitanía General de Guatemala), a subdivision of the [Viceroyalty of New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain"). This administrative unit encompassed present-day Guatemala, [Belize](/wiki/Belize "Belize"), [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador"), [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras"), [Nicaragua](/wiki/Nicaragua "Nicaragua"), [Costa Rica](/wiki/Costa_Rica "Costa Rica"), and the [Mexican](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") state of [Chiapas](/wiki/Chiapas "Chiapas").[[30]](#cite_note-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33)

Due to its strategic location on the [Pacific coast](/wiki/Pacific_coast "Pacific coast"), Guatemala became integrated into the [Manila Galleon](/wiki/Manila_galleon "Manila galleon") trade network, which connected Spanish colonies in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") and [the Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") between 1565 and 1815.[[32]](#cite_note-:5-34) Goods such as silver, porcelain, silk, and spices passed through Guatemala en route between [Manila](/wiki/Manila "Manila") and [Acapulco](/wiki/Acapulco "Acapulco").[[33]](#cite_note-35)[[32]](#cite_note-:5-34)

In 1773, the [Santa Marta earthquakes](/wiki/1773_Guatemala_earthquake "1773 Guatemala earthquake") devastated [Antigua Guatemala](/wiki/Antigua_Guatemala "Antigua Guatemala"), leading to the relocation of the capital to its current site in the [Ermita Valley](/wiki/Guatemala_City "Guatemala City").[[34]](#cite_note-36)[[35]](#cite_note-37) The new city, officially founded in 1776, became known as [La Nueva Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemala_City "Guatemala City") de la Asunción (modern-day Guatemala City).[[36]](#cite_note-38)

### Independence and Central America (1821–1847)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Independence and Central America (1821–1847)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Independenciacentroamerica2.jpg/250px-Independenciacentroamerica2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Independenciacentroamerica2.jpg)

Guatemalan [Whites](/wiki/White_people "White people") rejoice upon learning about the declaration of independence from Spain on 15 September 1821.

On 15 September 1821, [Gabino Gainza Fernandez de Medrano](/wiki/Gabino_Ga%C3%ADnza "Gabino Gaínza") and the [Captaincy General of Guatemala](/wiki/Captaincy_General_of_Guatemala "Captaincy General of Guatemala") officially proclaimed its independence from Spain at a public meeting in Guatemala City. Independence from Spain was gained, and the Captaincy General of Guatemala [joined](/wiki/Central_America_under_Mexican_rule "Central America under Mexican rule") the [First Mexican Empire](/wiki/First_Mexican_Empire "First Mexican Empire") under [Agustín de Iturbide](/wiki/Agust%C3%ADn_de_Iturbide "Agustín de Iturbide").

Under the First Mexican Empire, Mexico reached its greatest territorial extent, stretching from northern California to the provinces of Central America (excluding Panama, which was then part of [Gran Colombia](/wiki/Gran_Colombia "Gran Colombia")), which had not initially approved becoming part of the Mexican Empire but joined the Empire shortly after their independence. This region was formally a part of the [Viceroyalty of New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") throughout the colonial period, but as a practical matter had been administered separately from Mexico. It was not until 1825 that Guatemala created its own flag.[[37]](#cite_note-39)

In 1838, the liberal forces of Honduran leader [Francisco Morazán](/wiki/Francisco_Moraz%C3%A1n "Francisco Morazán") and Guatemalan [José Francisco Barrundia](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Francisco_Barrundia "José Francisco Barrundia") invaded Guatemala and reached San Sur, where they executed Chúa Alvarez, father-in-law of [Rafael Carrera](/wiki/Rafael_Carrera "Rafael Carrera"), then a military commander and later the first president of Guatemala. The liberal forces impaled Alvarez's head on a pike as a warning to followers of the Guatemalan [caudillo](/wiki/Caudillo "Caudillo").[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison200884–85-40) Carrera and his wife Petrona – who had come to confront Morazán as soon as they learned of the invasion and were in [Mataquescuintla](/wiki/Mataquescuintla "Mataquescuintla") – swore they would never forgive Morazán even in his grave; they felt it impossible to respect anyone who would not avenge family members.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison200885-41)

After sending several envoys, whom Carrera would not receive – and especially not Barrundia whom Carrera did not want to murder in cold blood – Morazán began a scorched-earth offensive, destroying villages in his path and stripping them of assets. The Carrera forces had to hide in the mountains.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison200886-42) Believing Carrera totally defeated, Morazán and Barrundia marched to [Guatemala City](/wiki/Guatemala_City "Guatemala City"), and were welcomed as saviors by state governor Pedro Valenzuela and members of the conservative [Aycinena clan](/w/index.php?title=Aycinena_clan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Aycinena clan (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clan_Aycinena "es:Clan Aycinena")], who proposed to sponsor one of the liberal battalions, while Valenzuela and Barrundia gave Morazán all the Guatemalan resources needed to solve any financial problem he had.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison200887-43) The [criollos](/wiki/Criollo_people "Criollo people") of both parties celebrated until dawn that they finally had a criollo caudillo like Morazán, who was able to crush the peasant rebellion.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison200888-44)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Federal_Republic_of_Central_America_%28orthographic_projection%29.svg/250px-Federal_Republic_of_Central_America_%28orthographic_projection%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Federal_Republic_of_Central_America_(orthographic_projection).svg)

The [Federal Republic of Central America](/wiki/Federal_Republic_of_Central_America "Federal Republic of Central America") (1823–1838) with its capital in Guatemala City

Morazán used the proceeds to support Los Altos and then replaced Valenzuela with [Mariano Rivera Paz](/wiki/Mariano_Rivera_Paz "Mariano Rivera Paz"), a member of the Aycinena clan, although he did not return to that clan any property confiscated in 1829. In revenge, [Juan José de Aycinena y Piñol](/wiki/Juan_Jos%C3%A9_de_Aycinena_y_Pi%C3%B1ol "Juan José de Aycinena y Piñol") voted to dissolve the [Central American Federation](/wiki/Central_American_Federation "Central American Federation") in [San Salvador](/wiki/San_Salvador "San Salvador") a little later, forcing Morazán to return to El Salvador to fight for his federal mandate. Along the way, Morazán increased repression in eastern Guatemala, as punishment for helping Carrera.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison200889-45) Knowing that Morazán had gone to El Salvador, Carrera tried to take [Salamá](/wiki/Salam%C3%A1 "Salamá") with the small force that remained, but was defeated, and lost his brother Laureano in combat. With just a few men left, he managed to escape, badly wounded, to [Sanarate](/wiki/Sanarate "Sanarate").[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison200891–92-46) After recovering somewhat, he attacked a detachment in [Jutiapa](/wiki/Jutiapa "Jutiapa") and got a small amount of booty which he gave to the volunteers who accompanied him. He then prepared to attack [Petapa](/wiki/Petapa "Petapa") near Guatemala City, where he was victorious, although with heavy casualties.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison200892-47)

In September of that year, Carrera attempted an assault on the capital of Guatemala, but the liberal general [Carlos Salazar Castro](/wiki/Carlos_Salazar_Castro "Carlos Salazar Castro") defeated him in the fields of [Villa Nueva](/wiki/Villa_Nueva,_Guatemala "Villa Nueva, Guatemala") and Carrera had to retreat.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHernández_de_León195920_April-48) After unsuccessfully trying to take [Quetzaltenango](/wiki/Quetzaltenango "Quetzaltenango"), Carrera found himself both surrounded and wounded. He had to capitulate to Mexican General [Agustín Guzmán](/wiki/Agust%C3%ADn_Guzm%C3%A1n "Agustín Guzmán"), who had been in Quetzaltenango since [Vicente Filísola](/wiki/Vicente_Fil%C3%ADsola "Vicente Filísola")'s arrival in 1823. Morazán had the opportunity to shoot Carrera, but did not, because he needed the support of the Guatemalan peasants to counter the attacks of [Francisco Ferrera](/wiki/Francisco_Ferrera "Francisco Ferrera") in [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador"). Instead, Morazán left Carrera in charge of a small fort in Mita, without any weapons. Knowing that Morazán was going to attack El Salvador, Francisco Ferrera gave arms and ammunition to Carrera and convinced him to attack Guatemala City.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison200896-49)

Meanwhile, despite insistent advice to definitively crush Carrera and his forces, Salazar tried to negotiate with him diplomatically; he even went as far as to show that he neither feared nor distrusted Carrera by removing the fortifications of the Guatemalan capital, in place since the battle of Villa Nueva.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHernández_de_León195920_April-48) Taking advantage of Salazar's good faith and Ferrera's weapons, Carrera took Guatemala City by surprise on 13 April 1839; Salazar, [Mariano Gálvez](/wiki/Mariano_G%C3%A1lvez "Mariano Gálvez") and Barrundia fled before the arrival of Carrera's militiamen. Salazar, in his nightshirt, vaulted roofs of neighboring houses and sought refuge,[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHernández_de_León195948-50)[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison2008122–127-51) reaching the border disguised as a peasant.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHernández_de_León195948-50)[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison2008122–127-51) With Salazar gone, Carrera reinstated Rivera Paz as head of state.

Between 1838 and 1840 a [secessionist movement](/wiki/Los_Altos,_Central_America "Los Altos, Central America") in the city of [Quetzaltenango](/wiki/Quetzaltenango "Quetzaltenango") founded the breakaway state of [Los Altos](/wiki/Los_Altos_(country) "Los Altos (country)") and sought independence from Guatemala. The most important members of the Liberal Party of Guatemala and liberal enemies of the conservative régime moved to Los Altos, leaving their exile in El Salvador.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHernández_de_León1959?[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_April_2021]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (April_2021)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-52) The liberals in Los Altos began severely criticizing the Conservative government of Rivera Paz.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHernández_de_León1959?[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_April_2021]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (April_2021)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-52) Los Altos was the region with the main production and economic activity of the former state of Guatemala. Without Los Altos, conservatives lost many of the resources that had given Guatemala hegemony in Central America.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHernández_de_León1959?[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_April_2021]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (April_2021)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-52) The government of Guatemala tried to reach a peaceful solution, but two years of bloody conflict followed.

On 17 April 1839, Guatemala declared itself independent from the [United Provinces of Central America](/wiki/Federal_Republic_of_Central_America "Federal Republic of Central America"). In 1840, Belgium began to act as an external source of support for Carrera's independence movement, in an effort to exert influence in Central America. The *Compagnie belge de colonisation* (Belgian Colonization Company), commissioned by Belgian [King Leopold I](/wiki/Leopold_I_of_Belgium "Leopold I of Belgium"), became the administrator of [Santo Tomas de Castilla](/wiki/Santo_Tomas_de_Castilla "Santo Tomas de Castilla")[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECompagnie_Belge_de_Colonisation1844-53) replacing the failed British [Eastern Coast of Central America Commercial and Agricultural Company](/wiki/Eastern_Coast_of_Central_America_Commercial_and_Agricultural_Company "Eastern Coast of Central America Commercial and Agricultural Company").[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECompagnie_Belge_de_Colonisation1844-53) Even though the colony eventually crumbled, Belgium continued to support Carrera in the mid-19th century, although Britain continued to be the main business and political partner to Carrera.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward1993498-54) Rafael Carrera was elected Guatemalan Governor in 1844.

### Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Republic")]

On 21 March 1847, Guatemala declared itself an independent republic and Carrera became its first president.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Guatemala_Real_1847_Silver_Coin_Fonrobert_7236.jpg/250px-Guatemala_Real_1847_Silver_Coin_Fonrobert_7236.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guatemala_Real_1847_Silver_Coin_Fonrobert_7236.jpg)

Proclamation Coin 1847 of the independent Republic of Guatemala

#### Carrera government (1847–1851)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Carrera government (1847–1851)")]

During the first term as president, Carrera brought the country back from extreme conservatism to a traditional moderation; in 1848, the liberals were able to drive him from office, after the country had been in turmoil for several months.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHernández_de_León1930-55)[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiceli197472-56) Carrera resigned of his own free will and left for México. The new liberal regime allied itself with the Aycinena family and swiftly passed a law ordering Carrera's execution if he returned to Guatemalan soil.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHernández_de_León1930-55)

The liberal criollos from [Quetzaltenango](/wiki/Quetzaltenango "Quetzaltenango") were led by general [Agustín Guzmán](/wiki/Agust%C3%ADn_Guzm%C3%A1n "Agustín Guzmán") who occupied the city after Corregidor general [Mariano Paredes](/wiki/Mariano_Paredes_(Guatemala) "Mariano Paredes (Guatemala)") was called to [Guatemala City](/wiki/Guatemala_City "Guatemala City") to take over the presidential office.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison2008270-57) They declared on 26 August 1848 that Los Altos was an independent state once again. The new state had the support of [Doroteo Vasconcelos](/wiki/Doroteo_Vasconcelos "Doroteo Vasconcelos")' régime in [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador") and the rebel guerrilla army of Vicente and Serapio Cruz, who were sworn enemies of Carrera.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison2008270–271-58) The interim government was led by Guzmán himself and had Florencio Molina and the priest Fernando Davila as his Cabinet members.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison2008271-59) On 5 September 1848, the criollos altenses chose a formal government led by Fernando Antonio Martínez.

In the meantime, Carrera decided to return to Guatemala and did so, entering at [Huehuetenango](/wiki/Huehuetenango "Huehuetenango"), where he met with native leaders and told them that they must remain united to prevail; the leaders agreed and slowly the segregated native communities started developing a new Indian identity under Carrera's leadership.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison2008275-60) In the meantime, in the eastern part of Guatemala, the [Jalapa](/wiki/Jalapa "Jalapa") region became increasingly dangerous; former president [Mariano Rivera Paz](/wiki/Mariano_Rivera_Paz "Mariano Rivera Paz") and rebel leader Vicente Cruz were both murdered there after trying to take over the Corregidor office in 1849.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison2008275-60)

When Carrera arrived to [Chiantla](/wiki/Chiantla "Chiantla") in [Huehuetenango](/wiki/Huehuetenango "Huehuetenango"), he received two altenses emissaries who told him that their soldiers were not going to fight his forces because that would lead to a native revolt, much like that of 1840; their only request from Carrera was to keep the natives under control.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison2008275-60) The altenses did not comply, and led by Guzmán and his forces, they started chasing Carrera; the caudillo hid, helped by his native allies and remained under their protection when the forces of [Miguel Garcia Granados](/wiki/Miguel_Garcia_Granados "Miguel Garcia Granados") arrived from [Guatemala City](/wiki/Guatemala_City "Guatemala City") looking for him.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison2008275-60)

On learning that officer [José Víctor Zavala](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_V%C3%ADctor_Zavala "José Víctor Zavala") had been appointed as Corregidor in Suchitepéquez, Carrera and his hundred [jacalteco](/wiki/Jakaltek_people "Jakaltek people") bodyguards crossed a dangerous jungle infested with [jaguars](/wiki/Jaguar "Jaguar") to meet his former friend. Zavala not only did not capture him, he agreed to serve under his orders, thus sending a strong message to both liberal and conservatives in Guatemala City that they would have to negotiate with Carrera or battle on two fronts – Quetzaltenango and Jalapa.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison2008278-61) Carrera went back to the Quetzaltenango area, while Zavala remained in Suchitepéquez as a tactical maneuver.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison2008-62) Carrera received a visit from a cabinet member of Paredes and told him that he had control of the native population and that he assured Paredes that he would keep them appeased.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison2008278-61) When the emissary returned to Guatemala City, he told the president everything Carrera said, and added that the native forces were formidable.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison2008279-63)

Guzmán went to [Antigua](/wiki/Antigua,_Guatemala "Antigua, Guatemala") to meet with another group of Paredes emissaries; they agreed that Los Altos would rejoin Guatemala, and that the latter would help Guzmán defeat his enemy and also build a port on the Pacific Ocean.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison2008279-63) Guzmán was sure of victory this time, but his plan evaporated when in his absence Carrera and his native allies occupied Quetzaltenango; Carrera appointed Ignacio Yrigoyen as [Corregidor](/wiki/Corregidor_(position) "Corregidor (position)") and convinced him that he should work with the K'iche', Q'anjobal and [Mam](/wiki/Mam_people "Mam people") leaders to keep the region under control.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison2008280-64) On his way out, Yrigoyen murmured to a friend: "Now he is the king of the Indians, indeed!"[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison2008280-64)

Guzmán then left for Jalapa, where he struck a deal with the rebels, while [Luis Batres Juarros](/wiki/Luis_Batres_Juarros "Luis Batres Juarros") convinced President Paredes to deal with Carrera. Back in Guatemala City within a few months, Carrera was commander-in-chief, backed by military and political support of the Indian communities from the densely populated western highlands.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeaver1999138-65) During the first presidency, from 1844 to 1848, he brought the country back from excessive conservatism to a moderate regime, and – with the advice of Juan José de Aycinena y Piñol and [Pedro de Aycinena](/wiki/Pedro_de_Aycinena "Pedro de Aycinena") – restored relations with the Church in Rome with a [Concordat](/wiki/Concordat_of_1854 "Concordat of 1854") ratified in 1854.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalvert198536-66)

#### Second Carrera government (1851–1865)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Second Carrera government (1851–1865)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Carrerayturcios_2014-06-22_09-46.jpg/250px-Carrerayturcios_2014-06-22_09-46.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carrerayturcios_2014-06-22_09-46.jpg)

Captain General [Rafael Carrera](/wiki/Rafael_Carrera "Rafael Carrera") after being appointed president for Life in 1854

After Carrera returned from exile in 1849 the president of El Salvador, [Doroteo Vasconcelos](/wiki/Doroteo_Vasconcelos "Doroteo Vasconcelos"), granted asylum to the Guatemalan liberals, who harassed the Guatemalan government in several different ways. José Francisco Barrundia established a liberal newspaper for that specific purpose. Vasconcelos supported a rebel faction named "La Montaña" in eastern Guatemala, providing and distributing money and weapons. By late 1850, Vasconcelos was getting impatient at the slow progress of the war with Guatemala and decided to plan an open attack. Under that circumstance, the Salvadorean head of state started a campaign against the conservative Guatemalan regime, inviting [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras") and [Nicaragua](/wiki/Nicaragua "Nicaragua") to participate in the alliance; only the [Honduran](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras") government led by [Juan Lindo](/wiki/Juan_Lindo "Juan Lindo") accepted.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHernández_de_León1930-55) In 1851 Guatemala defeated an Allied army from Honduras and El Salvador at the [Battle of La Arada](/wiki/Battle_of_La_Arada "Battle of La Arada").

In 1854 Carrera was declared "supreme and perpetual leader of the nation" for life, with the power to choose his successor. He held that position until he died on 14 April 1865. While he pursued some measures to set up a foundation for economic prosperity to please the conservative landowners, military challenges at home and a three-year war with Honduras, El Salvador, and Nicaragua dominated his presidency.

His rivalry with Gerardo Barrios, President of El Salvador, resulted in open war in 1863. At [Coatepeque](/wiki/Coatepeque,_Quetzaltenango "Coatepeque, Quetzaltenango") the Guatemalans suffered a [severe defeat](/wiki/Battle_of_Coatepeque "Battle of Coatepeque"), which was followed by a truce. Honduras joined with El Salvador, and Nicaragua and Costa Rica with Guatemala. The contest was finally settled in favor of Carrera, who besieged and occupied [San Salvador](/wiki/San_Salvador "San Salvador"), and dominated Honduras and Nicaragua. He continued to act in concert with the Clerical Party, and tried to maintain friendly relations with European governments. Before he died, Carrera nominated his friend and loyal soldier, Army Marshall [Vicente Cerna y Cerna](/wiki/Vicente_Cerna_y_Cerna "Vicente Cerna y Cerna"), as his successor.

#### Vicente Cerna y Cerna regime (1865–1871)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Vicente Cerna y Cerna regime (1865–1871)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/1d/VicenteCerna.jpg)](/wiki/File:VicenteCerna.jpg)

[Vicente Cerna y Cerna](/wiki/Vicente_Cerna_y_Cerna "Vicente Cerna y Cerna") was the president of Guatemala from 1865 to 1871.

Further information: [Vicente Cerna y Cerna](/wiki/Vicente_Cerna_y_Cerna "Vicente Cerna y Cerna")

[Vicente Cerna y Cerna](/wiki/Vicente_Cerna_y_Cerna "Vicente Cerna y Cerna") was [president of Guatemala](/wiki/President_of_Guatemala "President of Guatemala") from 24 May 1865 to 29 June 1871.[[65]](#cite_note-67) Liberal author [Alfonso Enrique Barrientos](/w/index.php?title=Alfonso_Enrique_Barrientos&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alfonso Enrique Barrientos (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alfonso_Enrique_Barrientos "es:Alfonso Enrique Barrientos")],[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarrientos1948106-68) described Marshall Cerna's government in the following manner:[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarrientos1948106-68)

> A conservative and archaic government, badly organized and with worse intentions, was in charge of the country, centralizing all powers in Vicente Cerna, ambitious military man, who not happy with the general rank, had promoted himself to the Army Marshall rank, even though that rank did not exist and it does not exist in the Guatemalan military. The Marshall called himself President of the Republic, but in reality he was the foreman of oppressed and savaged people, cowardly enough that they had not dared to tell the dictator to leave threatening him with a revolution.

The State and Church were a single unit, and the conservative régime was strongly allied to the power of [regular clergy](/wiki/Regular_clergy "Regular clergy") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), who were then among the largest landowners in Guatemala. The tight relationship between church and state had been ratified by the [Concordat of 1852](/wiki/Concordat_of_1854 "Concordat of 1854"), which was the law until Cerna was deposed in 1871.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAycinena18542–16-69) Even liberal generals like [Serapio Cruz](/w/index.php?title=Serapio_Cruz&action=edit&redlink=1 "Serapio Cruz (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Serapio_Cruz "es:Serapio Cruz")] realized that Rafael Carrera's political and military presence made him practically invincible. Thus the generals fought under his command,[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHernández_de_León1930-55) and waited—for a long time—until Carrera's death before beginning their revolt against the tamer Cerna.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Davison2008428-70) During Cerna's presidency, liberal party members were prosecuted and sent into exile; among them, those who started the Liberal Revolution of 1871.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHernández_de_León1930-55)

In 1871, the merchants guild, Consulado de Comercio, lost their exclusive court privilege. They had major effects on the economics of the time, and therefore land management. From 1839 to 1871, the Consulado held a consistent monopolistic position in the regime.[[69]](#cite_note-71)

#### Liberal governments (1871–1898)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Liberal governments (1871–1898)")]

Further information: [Justo Rufino Barrios](/wiki/Justo_Rufino_Barrios "Justo Rufino Barrios")

Guatemala's "Liberal Revolution" came in 1871 under the leadership of [Justo Rufino Barrios](/wiki/Justo_Rufino_Barrios "Justo Rufino Barrios"), who worked to modernize the country, improve trade, and introduce new crops and manufacturing. During this era coffee became an important crop for Guatemala.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster2000173–175-72) Barrios had ambitions of reuniting Central America and took the country to war in an unsuccessful attempt to attain it, losing his life on the battlefield in 1885 against forces in El Salvador.

[Manuel Barillas](/wiki/Manuel_Barillas "Manuel Barillas") was president from 16 March 1886 to 15 March 1892. Manuel Barillas was unique among liberal presidents of Guatemala between 1871 and 1944: he handed over power to his successor peacefully. When election time approached, he sent for the three Liberal candidates to ask them what their government plan would be.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDe_los_Ríos194878-73) Happy with what he heard from general [Reyna Barrios](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Reyna_Barrios "José María Reyna Barrios"),[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDe_los_Ríos194878-73) Barillas made sure that a huge column of Quetzaltenango and Totonicapán indigenous people came down from the mountains to vote for him. Reyna was elected president.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDe_los_Ríos194882-74)

[José María Reina Barrios](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Reina_Barrios "José María Reina Barrios") was president between 1892 and 1898. During Barrios's first term in office, the power of the landowners over the rural peasantry increased. He oversaw the rebuilding of parts of [Guatemala City](/wiki/Guatemala_City "Guatemala City") on a grander scale, with wide, Parisian-style avenues. He oversaw Guatemala hosting the first "[Exposición Centroamericana](/wiki/Exposici%C3%B3n_Centroamericana "Exposición Centroamericana")" ("Central American Fair") in 1897. During his second term, Barrios printed [bonds](/wiki/Bond_(finance) "Bond (finance)") to fund his ambitious plans, fueling [monetary inflation](/wiki/Monetary_inflation "Monetary inflation") and the rise of popular opposition to his regime.

His administration also worked on improving the roads, installing national and international telegraphs and introducing electricity to Guatemala City. Completing a transoceanic railway was a main objective of his government, with a goal to attract international investors at a time when the [Panama Canal](/wiki/Panama_Canal "Panama Canal") was not yet built.

#### Manuel Estrada Cabrera regime (1898–1920)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Manuel Estrada Cabrera regime (1898–1920)")]

Main article: [Manuel Estrada Cabrera](/wiki/Manuel_Estrada_Cabrera "Manuel Estrada Cabrera")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Estrada_Cabrera%2C_16_yrs._Pres._of_Guatemala_LCCN2016821114_%28cropped%29.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Estrada_Cabrera%2C_16_yrs._Pres._of_Guatemala_LCCN2016821114_%28cropped%29.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Estrada_Cabrera,_16_yrs._Pres._of_Guatemala_LCCN2016821114_(cropped).tif)

[Manuel Estrada Cabrera](/wiki/Manuel_Estrada_Cabrera "Manuel Estrada Cabrera") governed Guatemala between 1898 and 1920.

After the assassination of general [José María Reina Barrios](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Reina_Barrios "José María Reina Barrios") on 8 February 1898, the Guatemalan cabinet called an emergency meeting to appoint a new successor, but declined to invite Estrada Cabrera to the meeting, even though he was the designated successor to the presidency. There are two different descriptions of how Cabrera was able to become president. The first states that Cabrera entered the cabinet meeting "with pistol drawn" to assert his entitlement to the presidency,[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChapman200754-75) while the second states that he showed up unarmed to the meeting and demanded the presidency by virtue of being the designated successor.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArévalo_Martinez194542-76)

The first civilian Guatemalan head of state in over 50 years, Estrada Cabrera overcame resistance to his regime by August 1898 and called for elections in September, which he won handily.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArévalo_Martinez194546-77) In 1898 the legislature convened for the election of President Estrada Cabrera, who triumphed thanks to the large number of soldiers and policemen who went to vote in civilian clothes and to the large number of illiterate family that they brought with them to the polls.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETorres_Espinoza200742-78)

One of Estrada Cabrera's most famous and most bitter legacies was allowing the entry of the [United Fruit Company](/wiki/United_Fruit_Company "United Fruit Company") (UFCO) into the Guatemalan economic and political arena. As a member of the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberalism_in_Latin_America "Liberalism in Latin America"), he sought to encourage development of the nation's infrastructure of [highways](/wiki/Highway "Highway"), [railroads](/wiki/Railroad "Railroad"), and [sea ports](/wiki/Sea_port "Sea port") for the sake of expanding the export economy. By the time Estrada Cabrera assumed the presidency there had been repeated efforts to construct a railroad from the major port of [Puerto Barrios](/wiki/Puerto_Barrios "Puerto Barrios") to the capital, Guatemala City. Owing to lack of funding exacerbated by the collapse of the internal coffee trade, the railway fell 100 kilometres (60 mi) short of its goal. Estrada Cabrera decided, without consulting the legislature or judiciary, that striking a deal with the UFCO was the only way to finish the railway.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDosal1993-79) Cabrera signed a contract with UFCO's [Minor Cooper Keith](/wiki/Minor_Cooper_Keith "Minor Cooper Keith") in 1904 that gave the company tax exemptions, land grants, and control of all railroads on the Atlantic side.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChapman2007-80)

In 1906 Estrada faced serious revolts against his rule; the rebels were supported by the governments of some of the other [Central American](/wiki/Central_America "Central America") nations, but Estrada succeeded in putting them down. Elections were held by the people against the will of Estrada Cabrera and thus he had the president-elect murdered in retaliation. In 1907 Estrada narrowly survived an assassination attempt when a bomb exploded near his carriage.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Aerenlund2006-81) It has been suggested that the extreme despotic characteristics of Estrada did not emerge until after an attempt on his life in 1907.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArévalo_Martinez1945146-82)

[Guatemala City](/wiki/Guatemala_City "Guatemala City") was badly damaged in the [1917 Guatemala earthquake](/wiki/1917_Guatemala_earthquake "1917 Guatemala earthquake").

Estrada Cabrera continued in power until forced to resign after new revolts in 1920. By that time his power had declined drastically and he was reliant upon the loyalty of a few generals. While the United States threatened intervention if he was removed through revolution, a bipartisan coalition came together to remove him from the presidency. He was removed from office after the national assembly charged that he was mentally incompetent, and appointed [Carlos Herrera](/wiki/Carlos_Herrera "Carlos Herrera") in his place on 8 April 1920.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDosal199327-83)

Guatemala joined with El Salvador and Honduras in the Federation of Central America from 9 September 1921 until 14 January 1922.

Carlos Herrera served as President of Guatemala from 1920 until 1921. He was succeeded by [José María Orellana](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Orellana "José María Orellana"), who served from 1921 until 1926. [Lázaro Chacón González](/wiki/L%C3%A1zaro_Chac%C3%B3n_Gonz%C3%A1lez "Lázaro Chacón González") then served until 1931.

#### Jorge Ubico regime (1931–1944)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Jorge Ubico regime (1931–1944)")]

Main article: [Jorge Ubico](/wiki/Jorge_Ubico "Jorge Ubico")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Presidente_Jorge_Ubico_Casta%C3%B1eda.png/250px-Presidente_Jorge_Ubico_Casta%C3%B1eda.png)](/wiki/File:Presidente_Jorge_Ubico_Casta%C3%B1eda.png)

Jorge Ubico in 1931

The [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") began in 1929 and badly damaged the Guatemalan economy, causing a rise in [unemployment](/wiki/Unemployment "Unemployment"), and leading to unrest among workers and laborers. Afraid of a popular revolt, the Guatemalan landed elite lent their support to [Jorge Ubico](/wiki/Jorge_Ubico "Jorge Ubico"), who had become well known for "efficiency and cruelty" as a provincial governor. Ubico won the election that followed in 1931, in which he was the only candidate.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForster200112–15-84)[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGleijeses199110–11-85) After his election his policies quickly became authoritarian. He replaced the system of debt [peonage](/wiki/Peon "Peon") with a brutally enforced [vagrancy](/wiki/Vagrancy_(people) "Vagrancy (people)") law, requiring all men of working age who did not own land to work a minimum of 100 days of hard labor.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForster200129-86) His government used unpaid Indian labor to build roads and railways. Ubico also froze wages at very low levels, and passed a law allowing land-owners complete immunity from prosecution for any action they took to defend their property,[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForster200129-86) an action described by historians as legalizing murder.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGleijeses199113-87) He greatly strengthened the police force, turning it into one of the most efficient and ruthless in Latin America.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGleijeses199117-88) He gave them greater authority to shoot and imprison people suspected of breaking the labor laws. These laws created tremendous resentment against him among agricultural laborers.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForster200129–32-89) The government became highly militarized; under his rule, every provincial governor was a general in the army.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGleijeses199114-90)

Ubico continued his predecessor's policy of making massive concessions to the United Fruit Company, often at a cost to Guatemala. He granted the company 200,000 hectares (490,000 acres) of public land in exchange for a promise to build a port, a promise he later waived.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGleijeses199122-91) Since its entry into Guatemala, the UFCO had expanded its land-holdings by displacing farmers and converting their farmland to [banana plantations](/wiki/Banana_plantation "Banana plantation"). This process accelerated under Ubico's presidency, with the government doing nothing to stop it.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForster200119-92) The company received import duty and real estate tax exemptions from the government and controlled more land than any other individual or group. It also controlled the sole railroad in the country, the sole facilities capable of producing electricity, and the port facilities at [Puerto Barrios](/wiki/Puerto_Barrios "Puerto Barrios") on the Atlantic coast.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchlesingerKinzer199967–71-93)

Ubico saw the United States as an ally against the supposed communist threat of Mexico, and made efforts to gain its support. When the US [declared war](/wiki/United_States_declaration_of_war_upon_Germany_(1941) "United States declaration of war upon Germany (1941)") against Germany in 1941, Ubico acted on American instructions and arrested all people in Guatemala of [German descent](/wiki/German_Guatemalan "German Guatemalan").[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGleijeses199120-94) He also permitted the US to establish an air base in Guatemala, with the stated aim of protecting the [Panama Canal](/wiki/Panama_Canal "Panama Canal").[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEImmerman198237-95) However, Ubico was an admirer of European [fascists](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism"), such as [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco") and [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini"),[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGleijeses199119-96) and considered himself to be "another [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte")".[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDe_los_Ríos194898-97) He occasionally compared himself to [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler").[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShillington200238–39-98) He dressed ostentatiously and surrounded himself with statues and paintings of Napoleon, regularly commenting on the similarities between their appearances. He militarized numerous political and social institutions—including the post office, schools, and symphony orchestras—and placed military officers in charge of many government posts.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStreeter200011–12-99)[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEImmerman198232-100)[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrandin2000195-101)[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenz199616–17-102)[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELovemanDavies1997118–120-103)

#### Guatemalan Revolution (1944–1954)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Guatemalan Revolution (1944–1954)")]

Main article: [Guatemalan Revolution](/wiki/Guatemalan_Revolution "Guatemalan Revolution")

On 1 July 1944 Ubico was forced to resign from the presidency in response to a wave of protests and a [general strike](/wiki/General_strike "General strike") inspired by brutal labor conditions among plantation workers.[[102]](#cite_note-104) His chosen replacement, General [Juan Federico Ponce Vaides](/wiki/Juan_Federico_Ponce_Vaides "Juan Federico Ponce Vaides"), was forced out of office on 20 October 1944 by a [coup d'état](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "Coup d'état") led by Major [Francisco Javier Arana](/wiki/Francisco_Javier_Arana "Francisco Javier Arana") and Captain [Jacobo Árbenz Guzmán](/wiki/Jacobo_%C3%81rbenz_Guzm%C3%A1n "Jacobo Árbenz Guzmán"). About 100 people were killed in the coup. The country was then led by a [military junta](/wiki/Military_junta "Military junta") made up of Arana, Árbenz, and [Jorge Toriello Garrido](/wiki/Jorge_Toriello_Garrido "Jorge Toriello Garrido").[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForster200189–91-105)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Jacoboarbenz1950.jpg/250px-Jacoboarbenz1950.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacoboarbenz1950.jpg)

[Jacobo Árbenz](/wiki/Jacobo_%C3%81rbenz "Jacobo Árbenz"), president 1950-1954

The junta organized Guatemala's first free election, which the philosophically conservative writer and teacher [Juan José Arévalo](/wiki/Juan_Jos%C3%A9_Ar%C3%A9valo "Juan José Arévalo"), who wanted to turn the country into a [liberal](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism") [capitalist](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism") society won with a majority of 86%.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStreeter200012–15-106) His "[Christian Socialist](/wiki/Christian_socialism "Christian socialism")" policies were inspired to a large extent by the US [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") of President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression").[[105]](#cite_note-Chomsky_1985_154–160-107) Arévalo built new health centers, increased funding for education, and drafted a more liberal labor law,[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStreeter200014–15-108) while criminalizing unions in workplaces with less than 500 workers,[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForster200198–99-109) and cracking down on communists.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStreeter200015–16-110) Although Arévalo was popular among nationalists, he had enemies in the church and the military, and faced at least 25 coup attempts during his presidency.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStreeter200016–17-111)

Arévalo was constitutionally prohibited from contesting the 1950 elections. The largely free and fair elections were won by [Jacobo Árbenz Guzmán](/wiki/Jacobo_%C3%81rbenz_Guzm%C3%A1n "Jacobo Árbenz Guzmán"), Arévalo's defense minister.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGleijeses199173–84-112) Árbenz continued the moderate capitalist approach of Arévalo.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStreeter200018–19-113) His most important policy was [Decree 900](/wiki/Decree_900 "Decree 900"), a sweeping agrarian reform bill passed in 1952.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEImmerman198264–67-114)[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGleijeses1991144–146-115) Decree 900 transferred uncultivated land to landless peasants.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEImmerman198264–67-114) Only 1,710 of the nearly 350,000 private land-holdings were affected by the law,[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGleijeses1991149–164-116) which benefited approximately 500,000 individuals, or one-sixth of the population.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGleijeses1991149–164-116)

#### Coup and civil war (1954–1996)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Coup and civil war (1954–1996)")]

Main articles: [1954 Guatemalan coup d'état](/wiki/1954_Guatemalan_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1954 Guatemalan coup d'état"), [Guatemalan Civil War](/wiki/Guatemalan_Civil_War "Guatemalan Civil War"), and [Guatemalan genocide](/wiki/Guatemalan_genocide "Guatemalan genocide")

See also: [United States involvement in regime change](/wiki/United_States_involvement_in_regime_change "United States involvement in regime change")

Despite their popularity within the country, the reforms of the Guatemalan Revolution were disliked by the United States government, which was predisposed by the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") to see it as communist, and by the UFCO, whose hugely profitable business had been affected by the end to brutal labor practices.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStreeter200015–16-110)[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEImmerman198248–50-117) The attitude of the US government was also influenced by a propaganda campaign carried out by the UFCO.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaterson2009304-118)

US President [Harry Truman](/wiki/Harry_Truman "Harry Truman") authorized [Operation PBFortune](/wiki/Operation_PBFortune "Operation PBFortune") to topple Árbenz in 1952, with the support of Nicaraguan dictator [Anastasio Somoza García](/wiki/Anastasio_Somoza_Garc%C3%ADa "Anastasio Somoza García"),[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchlesingerKinzer1999102-119) but the operation was aborted when too many details became public.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchlesingerKinzer1999102-119)[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGleijeses1991228–231-120) [Dwight D. Eisenhower](/wiki/Dwight_D._Eisenhower "Dwight D. Eisenhower") was elected US president in 1952, promising to take a harder line against communism; the close links that his staff members [John Foster Dulles](/wiki/John_Foster_Dulles "John Foster Dulles") and [Allen Dulles](/wiki/Allen_Dulles "Allen Dulles") had to the UFCO also predisposed him to act against Árbenz.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEImmerman1982122–127-121) Eisenhower authorized the CIA to carry out Operation PBSuccess in August 1953. The CIA armed, funded, and trained a force of 480 men led by [Carlos Castillo Armas](/wiki/Carlos_Castillo_Armas "Carlos Castillo Armas").[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEImmerman1982161–170-122)[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchlesingerKinzer1999171–175-123) The force invaded Guatemala on 18 June 1954, backed by a heavy campaign of [psychological warfare](/wiki/Psychological_warfare "Psychological warfare"), including bombings of [Guatemala City](/wiki/Guatemala_City "Guatemala City") and an anti-Árbenz radio station claiming to be genuine news.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEImmerman1982161–170-122) The invasion force fared poorly militarily, but the psychological warfare and the possibility of a US invasion intimidated the Guatemalan army, which refused to fight. Árbenz resigned on 27 June.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEImmerman1982173–178-124)[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchlesingerKinzer1999190–204-125)

Following negotiations in [San Salvador](/wiki/San_Salvador "San Salvador"), Carlos Castillo Armas became president on 7 July 1954.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEImmerman1982173–178-124) Elections were held in early October, from which all political parties were barred from participating. Castillo Armas was the only candidate and won the election with 99% of the vote.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEImmerman1982173–178-124) Castillo Armas reversed [Decree 900](/wiki/Decree_900 "Decree 900") and ruled until 26 July 1957, when he was assassinated by Romeo Vásquez, a member of his personal guard. After the rigged[[105]](#cite_note-Chomsky_1985_154–160-107) election that followed, General [Miguel Ydígoras Fuentes](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Miguel_Ram%C3%B3n_Yd%C3%ADgoras_Fuentes "José Miguel Ramón Ydígoras Fuentes") assumed power. He is celebrated for challenging the Mexican president to a gentleman's [duel](/wiki/Duel "Duel") on the bridge on the south border to end a feud on the subject of illegal fishing by Mexican boats on Guatemala's Pacific coast, two of which were sunk by the Guatemalan Air Force. Ydigoras authorized the training of 5,000 anti-[Castro](/wiki/Fidel_Castro "Fidel Castro") [Cubans](/wiki/Cubans "Cubans") in Guatemala. He also provided airstrips in the region of [Petén](/wiki/Pet%C3%A9n_(department) "Petén (department)") for what later became the US-sponsored, failed [Bay of Pigs Invasion](/wiki/Bay_of_Pigs_Invasion "Bay of Pigs Invasion") in 1961.

On 13 November 1960, a group of left-wing junior military officers of the *Escuela Politécnica* national military academy led a failed revolt against Ydigoras' government. The rebels fled to the mountains of eastern Guatemala and neighboring Honduras and formed MR-13 ([Movimiento Revolucionario 13 Noviembre](/wiki/Revolutionary_Movement_13th_November "Revolutionary Movement 13th November")). On 6 February 1962, in Bananera, they attacked the offices of the United Fruit Company. The attack sparked sympathetic strikes and university student [walkouts](/wiki/Walkout "Walkout") throughout the country, to which the government responded with a violent crackdown.[[124]](#cite_note-126)

In 1963, Ydígoras, despite the firm opposition of the [Kennedy administration](/wiki/Kennedy_administration "Kennedy administration"), had pledged to allow Arévalo return from exile and run in a free and open election. Arévalo returned on 27 March 1963 to announce his candidacy for the scheduled November presidential elections, however Ydigoras' government was ousted on 31 March 1963, when the Guatemalan Air Force attacked several military bases; the coup was led by his Defense Minister, Colonel [Enrique Peralta Azurdia](/wiki/Enrique_Peralta_Azurdia "Enrique Peralta Azurdia").[[125]](#cite_note-Rabe-127) The new régime intensified its counterinsurgency campaign against the guerrillas that had begun under Ydígoras-Fuentes.[[126]](#cite_note-128)

In 1966, [Julio César Méndez Montenegro](/wiki/Julio_C%C3%A9sar_M%C3%A9ndez_Montenegro "Julio César Méndez Montenegro") was elected president of Guatemala under the banner "Democratic Opening". Mendez Montenegro was the candidate of the Revolutionary Party, a center-left party that had its origins in the post-Ubico era. During this time, rightist [paramilitary](/wiki/Paramilitary "Paramilitary") organizations, such as the "White Hand" (*[Mano Blanca](/wiki/Mano_Blanca "Mano Blanca")*), and the Anticommunist Secret Army (*Ejército Secreto Anticomunista*) were formed. Those groups were the forerunners of the infamous "[Death Squads](/wiki/Death_squad "Death squad")". Military advisers from the [United States Army Special Forces](/wiki/Special_Forces_(United_States_Army) "Special Forces (United States Army)") (Green Berets) were sent to Guatemala to train Guatemala's armed forces and help transform it into a modern counter-insurgency force, which eventually made it the most sophisticated in Central America.[[127]](#cite_note-129)

In 1970, Colonel [Carlos Manuel Arana Osorio](/wiki/Carlos_Manuel_Arana_Osorio "Carlos Manuel Arana Osorio") was elected president. By 1972, members of the guerrilla movement entered the country from Mexico and settled in the Western Highlands. In the [disputed election of 1974](/wiki/Guatemalan_general_election,_1974 "Guatemalan general election, 1974"), General [Kjell Laugerud García](/wiki/Kjell_Eugenio_Laugerud_Garc%C3%ADa "Kjell Eugenio Laugerud García") defeated General [Efraín Ríos Montt](/wiki/Efra%C3%ADn_R%C3%ADos_Montt "Efraín Ríos Montt"), a candidate of the Christian Democratic Party, who claimed that he had been cheated out of a victory through fraud.

On 4 February 1976, a major earthquake destroyed several cities and caused more than 25,000 deaths, especially among the poor, whose housing was substandard. The government's failure to respond rapidly to the aftermath of the earthquake and to relieve homelessness gave rise to widespread discontent, which contributed to growing popular unrest. General [Romeo Lucas García](/wiki/Romeo_Lucas_Garc%C3%ADa "Romeo Lucas García") assumed power in 1978 in a fraudulent election.

The 1970s saw the rise of two new guerrilla organizations, the Guerrilla Army of the Poor (EGP) and the Organization of the People in Arms (ORPA). They began guerrilla attacks that included urban and rural warfare, mainly against the military and some civilian supporters of the army. The army and the paramilitary forces responded with a brutal counter-insurgency campaign that resulted in tens of thousands of civilian deaths.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaFeber1993165-130) In 1979, US President [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter"), who had until then been providing public support for the government forces, ordered a ban on all military aid to the Guatemalan Army because of its widespread and systematic abuse of human rights.[[105]](#cite_note-Chomsky_1985_154–160-107) However, documents have since come to light that suggest that American aid continued throughout the Carter years, through clandestine channels.[[129]](#cite_note-131)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Memorial_Rio_Negro.jpg/250px-Memorial_Rio_Negro.jpg)](/wiki/File:Memorial_Rio_Negro.jpg)

Memorial to the victims of the [Río Negro massacres](/wiki/R%C3%ADo_Negro_massacres "Río Negro massacres")

On 31 January 1980, a group of indigenous [K'iche'](/wiki/K%27iche%27_people "K'iche' people") took over the Spanish Embassy to protest army massacres in the countryside. The Guatemalan government armed forces launched an assault that killed almost everyone inside in a [fire that consumed the building](/wiki/Burning_of_the_Spanish_Embassy "Burning of the Spanish Embassy"). The Guatemalan government claimed that the activists set the fire, thus [immolating](/wiki/Self-immolation "Self-immolation") themselves.[[130]](#cite_note-132) However the Spanish ambassador survived the fire and disputed this claim, saying that the Guatemalan police intentionally killed almost everyone inside and set the fire to erase traces of their acts. As a result, the government of Spain broke off diplomatic relations with Guatemala.

This government was overthrown in 1982 and General [Efraín Ríos Montt](/wiki/Efra%C3%ADn_R%C3%ADos_Montt "Efraín Ríos Montt") was named president of the military junta. He continued the bloody campaign of torture, [forced disappearances](/wiki/Forced_disappearance "Forced disappearance"), and "[scorched earth](/wiki/Scorched_earth "Scorched earth")" warfare. The country became a [pariah state](/wiki/Pariah_state "Pariah state") internationally, although the regime received considerable support from the [Reagan Administration](/wiki/Reagan_Administration "Reagan Administration"),[[131]](#cite_note-133)[[132]](#cite_note-134) and Reagan himself described Ríos Montt as "a man of great personal integrity".[[133]](#cite_note-135) Ríos Montt was overthrown by General [Óscar Humberto Mejía Victores](/wiki/%C3%93scar_Humberto_Mej%C3%ADa_Victores "Óscar Humberto Mejía Victores"), who called for an election of a national constituent assembly to write a new constitution, leading to a free election in 1986, won by [Vinicio Cerezo Arévalo](/wiki/Marco_Vinicio_Cerezo_Ar%C3%A9valo "Marco Vinicio Cerezo Arévalo"), the candidate of the Christian Democracy Party.

In 1982, the four guerrilla groups, EGP, ORPA, FAR and PGT, merged and formed the [URNG](/wiki/Guatemalan_National_Revolutionary_Unity "Guatemalan National Revolutionary Unity"), influenced by the [Salvadoran](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador") guerrilla [FMLN](/wiki/Farabundo_Mart%C3%AD_National_Liberation_Front "Farabundo Martí National Liberation Front"), the [Nicaraguan](/wiki/Nicaragua "Nicaragua") [FSLN](/wiki/Sandinista_National_Liberation_Front "Sandinista National Liberation Front") and [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba")'s government, in order to become stronger. As a result of the Army's "scorched earth" tactics in the countryside, more than 45,000 Guatemalans fled across the border to Mexico. The Mexican government placed the refugees in camps in [Chiapas](/wiki/Chiapas "Chiapas") and [Tabasco](/wiki/Tabasco "Tabasco").

In 1992, the [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") was awarded to [Rigoberta Menchú](/wiki/Rigoberta_Mench%C3%BA "Rigoberta Menchú") for her efforts to bring international attention to the government-sponsored [genocide against the indigenous population](/wiki/Guatemalan_genocide "Guatemalan genocide").[[134]](#cite_note-136)

#### 1996–2000

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 1996–2000")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Chichicastenango_market.jpg/250px-Chichicastenango_market.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chichicastenango_market.jpg)

An outdoor market in [Chichicastenango](/wiki/Chichicastenango "Chichicastenango"), 2018

The [Guatemalan Civil War](/wiki/Guatemalan_Civil_War "Guatemalan Civil War") ended in 1996 with a peace accord between the guerrillas and the government, negotiated by the United Nations through intense brokerage by nations such as Norway and Spain. Both sides made major concessions. The guerrilla fighters disarmed and received land to work. According to the U.N.-sponsored [truth commission](/wiki/Truth_commission "Truth commission") (the [Commission for Historical Clarification](/wiki/Commission_for_Historical_Clarification "Commission for Historical Clarification")), government forces and state-sponsored, CIA-trained paramilitaries were responsible for over 93% of the human rights violations during the war.[[135]](#cite_note-RightsViolations-137)

In the last few years,[*[timeframe?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Relative_time_references "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] millions of documents related to crimes committed during the civil war have been found abandoned by the former Guatemalan police. The families of over 45,000 Guatemalan activists who [disappeared](/wiki/Forced_Disappearance "Forced Disappearance") during the civil war are now reviewing the documents, which have been digitized. This could lead to further legal actions.[[136]](#cite_note-PoliceDocuments-138)

During the first ten years of the civil war, the victims of the state-sponsored terror were primarily students, workers, professionals, and opposition figures, but in the last years they were thousands of mostly rural [Maya](/wiki/Maya_peoples "Maya peoples") farmers and non-combatants. More than 450 Maya villages were destroyed and over 1 million people became refugees or displaced within Guatemala.

In 1995, the Catholic Archdiocese of Guatemala began the Recovery of Historical Memory (REMHI) project,[[137]](#cite_note-139) known in Spanish as "El Proyecto de la Recuperación de la Memoria Histórica", to collect the facts and history of Guatemala's long civil war and confront the truth of those years. On 24 April 1998, REMHI presented the results of its work in the report "Guatemala: Nunca Más!". This report summarized testimony and statements of thousands of witnesses and victims of repression during the Civil War. "The report laid the blame for 80 per cent of the [atrocities](/wiki/Atrocity_crime "Atrocity crime") at the door of the Guatemalan Army and its collaborators within the social and political elite."[[138]](#cite_note-Stanford,_Peter_2008-140)

Catholic Bishop [Juan José Gerardi Conedera](/wiki/Juan_Jos%C3%A9_Gerardi_Conedera "Juan José Gerardi Conedera") worked on the Recovery of Historical Memory Project and two days after he announced the release of its report on victims of the Guatemalan Civil War, "Guatemala: Nunca Más!", in April 1998, Bishop Gerardi was attacked in his garage and beaten to death.[[138]](#cite_note-Stanford,_Peter_2008-140) In 2001, in the first trial in a civilian court of members of the military in Guatemalan history, three Army officers were convicted of his death and sentenced to 30 years in prison. A priest was convicted as an accomplice and was sentenced to 20 years in prison.[[139]](#cite_note-141)

According to the report, *Recuperación de la Memoria Histórica* (REMHI), some 200,000 people died. More than one million people were forced to flee their homes and hundreds of villages were destroyed. The Historical Clarification Commission attributed more than 93% of all documented violations of human rights to Guatemala's military government, and estimated that Maya Indians accounted for 83% of the victims. It concluded in 1999 that state actions constituted genocide.[[140]](#cite_note-142)[[141]](#cite_note-143)

In some areas such as [Baja Verapaz](/wiki/Baja_Verapaz "Baja Verapaz"), the Truth Commission found that the Guatemalan state engaged in an intentional policy of genocide against particular ethnic groups in the [Civil War](/wiki/Guatemalan_Civil_War "Guatemalan Civil War").[[135]](#cite_note-RightsViolations-137) In 1999, US President [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") said that the US had been wrong to have provided support to the Guatemalan military forces that took part in these brutal civilian killings.[[142]](#cite_note-Babington1999-144)

#### Since 2000

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Since 2000")]

Since the peace accords Guatemala has had both economic growth and successive democratic elections, most recently in 2023. In the 2023 elections, [Bernardo Arévalo](/wiki/Bernardo_Ar%C3%A9valo "Bernardo Arévalo") won the presidency. He assumed office in January 2024.

In January 2012 Efrain Rios Montt, the former dictator of Guatemala, appeared in a Guatemalan court on genocide charges. During the hearing, the government presented evidence of over 100 incidents involving at least 1,771 deaths, 1,445 rapes, and the displacement of nearly 30,000 Guatemalans during his 17-month rule from 1982 to 1983. The prosecution wanted him incarcerated because he was viewed as a flight risk but he remained free on bail, under house arrest and guarded by the Guatemalan National Civil Police (PNC). On 10 May 2013, Rios Montt was found guilty and sentenced to 80 years in prison. It marked the first time that a national court had found a former head of state guilty of genocide.[[143]](#cite_note-145) The conviction was later overturned, and Montt's trial resumed in January 2015.[[144]](#cite_note-146) In August 2015, a Guatemalan court ruled that Rios Montt could stand trial for genocide and crimes against humanity, but that he could not be sentenced due to his age and deteriorating health.[[145]](#cite_note-147)

Ex-President [Alfonso Portillo](/wiki/Alfonso_Portillo "Alfonso Portillo") was arrested in January 2010 while trying to flee Guatemala. He was acquitted in May 2010, by a panel of judges that threw out some of the evidence and discounted certain witnesses as unreliable.[[146]](#cite_note-icpor-148) The Guatemalan Attorney-General, [Claudia Paz y Paz](/wiki/Claudia_Paz_y_Paz "Claudia Paz y Paz"), called the verdict "a terrible message of injustice", and "a wake up call about the power structures". In its appeal, the [International Commission Against Impunity in Guatemala](/wiki/International_Commission_Against_Impunity_in_Guatemala "International Commission Against Impunity in Guatemala") (CICIG), a UN judicial group assisting the Guatemalan government, called the decision's assessment of the meticulously documented evidence against Portillo Cabrera "whimsical" and said the decision's assertion that the president of Guatemala and his ministers had no responsibility for handling public funds ran counter to the constitution and laws of Guatemala.[[147]](#cite_note-149) A New York grand jury had indicted Portillo Cabrera in 2009 for embezzlement; following his acquittal on those charges in Guatemala that country's [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_(Guatemala) "Supreme Court (Guatemala)") authorized his extradition to the US.[[148]](#cite_note-150)[[149]](#cite_note-151) The Guatemalan judiciary is deeply corrupt and the selection committee for new nominations has been captured by criminal elements.[[146]](#cite_note-icpor-148)

At the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics") in [London](/wiki/London "London"), Guatemala received its first-ever Olympic medal when [Erick Barrondo](/wiki/Erick_Barrondo "Erick Barrondo") won the [men's 20 kilometer walk](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_20_kilometres_walk "Athletics at the 2012 Summer Olympics – Men's 20 kilometres walk").[[150]](#cite_note-152)

##### Pérez Molina government and "La Línea"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Pérez Molina government and \"La Línea\"")]

Main article: [Otto Pérez Molina](/wiki/Otto_P%C3%A9rez_Molina "Otto Pérez Molina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/10_Aerial_view_-_Zona_14_-_Ciudad_de_Guatemala.jpg/250px-10_Aerial_view_-_Zona_14_-_Ciudad_de_Guatemala.jpg)](/wiki/File:10_Aerial_view_-_Zona_14_-_Ciudad_de_Guatemala.jpg)

[Guatemala City](/wiki/Guatemala_City "Guatemala City") is the capital and largest city of Guatemala and the most populous urban area in [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America").

Retired general [Otto Pérez Molina](/wiki/Otto_P%C3%A9rez_Molina "Otto Pérez Molina") was elected president in 2011 along with [Roxana Baldetti](/wiki/Roxana_Baldetti "Roxana Baldetti"), the first woman ever elected vice-president in Guatemala; they began their term in office on 14 January 2012.[[151]](#cite_note-153) But on 16 April 2015, a United Nations (UN) anti-corruption agency report implicated several high-profile politicians including Baldetti's private secretary, Juan Carlos Monzón, and the director of the Guatemalan Internal Revenue Service (SAT).[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*][[152]](#cite_note-veliz-154) The revelations provoked more public outrage than had been seen since the presidency of General [Kjell Eugenio Laugerud García](/wiki/Kjell_Eugenio_Laugerud_Garc%C3%ADa "Kjell Eugenio Laugerud García"). The [International Commission against Impunity in Guatemala](/wiki/International_Commission_against_Impunity_in_Guatemala "International Commission against Impunity in Guatemala") (CICIG) worked with the Guatemalan attorney-general to reveal the scam known as "[La Línea](/wiki/La_L%C3%ADnea_corruption_case "La Línea corruption case")", following a year-long investigation that included [wire taps](/wiki/Telephone_tapping "Telephone tapping").

Officials received bribes from importers in exchange for discounted import tariffs,[[152]](#cite_note-veliz-154) a practice rooted in a long tradition of customs corruption in the country, as a fund-raising tactic of successive military governments for counterinsurgency operations during Guatemala's 36-year-long [civil war](/wiki/Guatemala_Civil_War "Guatemala Civil War").[[153]](#cite_note-linea10junio-155)[[154]](#cite_note-Solano22abril-156)

A Facebook event using the hashtag *#RenunciaYa* (Resign Now) invited citizens to go downtown in [Guatemala City](/wiki/Guatemala_City "Guatemala City") to ask for Baldetti's resignation. Within days, over 10,000 people RSVPed that they would attend. Organizers made clear that no political party or group was behind the event, and instructed protesters at the event to follow the law.
