They also urged people to bring water, food and sunblock, but not to cover their faces or wear political party colors.[[155]](#cite_note-ollantay21mayo-157) Tens of thousands of people took to the streets of Guatemala City. They protested in front of the presidential palace. Baldetti resigned a few days later. She was forced to remain in Guatemala when the United States revoked her visa. The Guatemalan government arraigned her, since it had enough evidence to suspect her involvement in the "La Linea" scandal. The prominence of US Ambassador Todd Robinson in the Guatemalan political scene once the scandal broke led to the suspicion that the US government was behind the investigation, perhaps because it needed an honest government in Guatemala to counter the presence of China and Russia in the region.[[156]](#cite_note-porras2015-158)

The UN anti-corruption committee has reported on other cases since then, and more than 20 government officials have stepped down. Some were arrested. Two of those cases involved two former presidential private secretaries: Juan de Dios Rodríguez in the Guatemalan Social Service and Gustave Martínez, who was involved in a bribery scandal at the coal power plant company. [Jaguar Energy](/w/index.php?title=Jaguar_Energy&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jaguar Energy (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jaguar_Energy "es:Jaguar Energy")] Martínez was also Perez Molina's son-in-law.[[157]](#cite_note-159)

Leaders of the political opposition have also been implicated in CICIG investigations: several legislators and members of Libertad Democrática Renovada party (LIDER) were formally accused of bribery-related issues, prompting a large decline in the electoral prospects of its presidential candidate, Manuel Baldizón, who until April had been almost certain to become the next Guatemalan president in the 6 September 2015 presidential elections. Baldizón's popularity steeply declined and he filed accusations with the Organization of American States against CICIG leader Iván Velásquez of international obstruction in Guatemalan internal affairs.[[158]](#cite_note-160)

CICIG reported its cases so often on Thursdays that Guatemalans coined the term "CICIG Thursdays". But a Friday press conference brought the crisis to its peak: on Friday 21 August 2015, the CICIG and Attorney General Thelma Aldana presented enough evidence to convince the public that both President Pérez Molina and former vice President Baldetti were the actual leaders of "La Línea". Baldetti was arrested the same day and an impeachment was requested for the president. Several cabinet members resigned and the clamor for the president's resignation grew after Perez Molina defiantly assured the nation in a televised message broadcast on 23 August 2015 that he was not going to resign.[[159]](#cite_note-periodico21a-161)[[160]](#cite_note-eu23a-162)

Thousands of protesters took to the streets again, this time to demand the increasingly isolated president's resignation. Guatemala's Congress named a commission of five legislators to consider whether to remove the president's immunity from prosecution. The Supreme Court approved. A major day of action kicked off early on 27 August, with marches and roadblocks across the country. Urban groups who had spearheaded regular protests since the scandal broke in April, on the 27th sought to unite with the rural and indigenous organizations who orchestrated the road blocks.

The strike in Guatemala City was full of a diverse and peaceful crowd ranging from the indigenous poor to the well-heeled, and it included many students from public and private universities. Hundreds of schools and businesses closed in support of the protests. The *Comité Coordinador de Asociaciones Agrícolas, Comerciales, Industriales y Financieras* (CACIF) Guatemala's most powerful business leaders, issued a statement demanding that Pérez Molina step down, and urged Congress to withdraw his immunity from prosecution.[[161]](#cite_note-cacifMolina-163)

The attorney general's office released its own statement, calling for the president's resignation "to prevent ungovernability that could destabilize the nation". As pressure mounted, the president's former ministers of defense and of the interior, who had been named in the corruption investigation and resigned, abruptly left the country.[[162]](#cite_note-lopez27a-164) Pérez Molina meanwhile had been losing support by the day. The private sector called for his resignation; however, he also managed to get support from entrepreneurs that were not affiliated with the private sector chambers: Mario López Estrada – grandchild of former dictator [Manuel Estrada Cabrera](/wiki/Manuel_Estrada_Cabrera "Manuel Estrada Cabrera") and the billionaire owner of cellular phone companies – had some of his executives assume the vacated cabinet positions.[[163]](#cite_note-165)

The Guatemalan radio station Emisoras Unidas reported exchanging text messages with Perez Molina. Asked whether he planned to resign, he wrote: "I will face whatever is necessary to face, and what the law requires." Some protesters demanded the general election be postponed, both because of the crisis and because it was plagued with accusations of irregularities. Others warned that suspending the vote could lead to an institutional vacuum.[[164]](#cite_note-166) However, on 2 September 2015 Pérez Molina resigned, a day after Congress impeached him.[[165]](#cite_note-167)[[166]](#cite_note-168) On 3 September 2015 he was summoned to the Justice Department for his first legal audience for the [La Linea corruption case](/wiki/La_Linea_corruption_case "La Linea corruption case").[[167]](#cite_note-169)[[168]](#cite_note-170)

In June 2016 a United Nations-backed prosecutor described the administration of Pérez Molina as a crime syndicate and outlined another corruption case, this one dubbed *Cooperacha* (Kick-in). The head of the Social Security Institute and at least five other ministers pooled funds to buy Molina luxurious gifts such as motorboats, spending over $4.7 million in three years.[[169]](#cite_note-171)

##### Jimmy Morales and Alejandro Giammattei in power (2016–2024)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Jimmy Morales and Alejandro Giammattei in power (2016–2024)")]

In the [October 2015 presidential election](/wiki/2015_Guatemalan_general_election "2015 Guatemalan general election"), former TV comedian [Jimmy Morales](/wiki/Jimmy_Morales "Jimmy Morales") was elected as the new president of Guatemala after huge anti-corruption demonstrations. He took office in January 2016.[[170]](#cite_note-auto1-172)

In December 2017, President Morales announced that Guatemala will move its embassy in Israel to Jerusalem, becoming the first nation to follow the United States.[[171]](#cite_note-173)

In January 2020, [Alejandro Giammattei](/wiki/Alejandro_Giammattei "Alejandro Giammattei") replaced Jimmy Morales as the president of Guatemala. Giammattei had won the [presidential election](/wiki/2019_Guatemalan_general_election "2019 Guatemalan general election") in August 2019 with his "tough-on-crime" agenda.[[172]](#cite_note-auto-174)

In November 2020, large [protests and demonstrations](/wiki/2020_Guatemalan_protests "2020 Guatemalan protests") occurred in Guatemala against President Alejandro Giammattei and the legislature, because of cutting educational and health spending.[[173]](#cite_note-175)

In August 2023, [Bernardo Arévalo](/wiki/Bernardo_Ar%C3%A9valo "Bernardo Arévalo"), the candidate of the center-left [Semilla](/wiki/Semilla_(political_party) "Semilla (political party)") (Seed) Movement, had a landslide victory in Guatemala's [presidential election](/wiki/2023_Guatemalan_general_election "2023 Guatemalan general election").[[174]](#cite_note-176) The outgoing Giammattei administration attempted to control the outcome of the election by cracking down on journalists, anti-corruption investigators and social movements. After Arévalo's victory, the outgoing government attempted to prevent him from taking power. However, indigenous-led protests and international pressure forced the government to accept the election results.[[175]](#cite_note-:4-177)

##### Bernardo Arévalo and Movimiento Semilla (2024–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Bernardo Arévalo and Movimiento Semilla (2024–present)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Retrato_oficial_del_Presidente_de_la_Rep%C3%BAblica_Bernardo_Ar%C3%A9valo_de_Le%C3%B3n%2C_como_Comandante_General_de_las_Fuerzas_Armadas_de_Guatemala.png/250px-Retrato_oficial_del_Presidente_de_la_Rep%C3%BAblica_Bernardo_Ar%C3%A9valo_de_Le%C3%B3n%2C_como_Comandante_General_de_las_Fuerzas_Armadas_de_Guatemala.png)](/wiki/File:Retrato_oficial_del_Presidente_de_la_Rep%C3%BAblica_Bernardo_Ar%C3%A9valo_de_Le%C3%B3n,_como_Comandante_General_de_las_Fuerzas_Armadas_de_Guatemala.png)

[Bernardo Arévalo](/wiki/Bernardo_Ar%C3%A9valo "Bernardo Arévalo"), president of Guatemala

[Bernardo Arévalo](/wiki/Bernardo_Ar%C3%A9valo "Bernardo Arévalo") is the son of the former president [Juan José Arévalo](/wiki/Juan_Jos%C3%A9_Ar%C3%A9valo "Juan José Arévalo"), who was the first democratically chosen president of Guatemala. Arévalo was scheduled to assume the role as the 52nd president of Guatemala with leadership of [Semilla](/wiki/Semilla_(political_party) "Semilla (political party)") on 14 January 2024,[[176]](#cite_note-178) however, his inauguration would be delayed due to the failure of the event's commission to approve a congressional delegation.[[177]](#cite_note-179)[[178]](#cite_note-180)

Minutes after midnight, he was finally sworn in as Guatemala's president on 15 January.[[179]](#cite_note-181)
His campaign promotes anti-corruption and economic opportunities for Guatemalans.[[180]](#cite_note-182) In his first few days in office, Arévalo reversed a government agreement signed by his [predecessor](/wiki/Alejandro_Giammattei "Alejandro Giammattei") that would have granted security and vehicles to former officials from the [Giammattei cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Alejandro_Giammattei "Cabinet of Alejandro Giammattei") for six years.[[181]](#cite_note-183)[[182]](#cite_note-184)

On 8 February 2024, Arévalo and Francisco Jiménez, the [Minister of the Interior](/wiki/Ministry_of_the_Interior_(Guatemala) "Ministry of the Interior (Guatemala)"), announced the creation of the Special Group Against Extortion (GECE), a special force within the [National Civil Police](/wiki/National_Civil_Police_(Guatemala) "National Civil Police (Guatemala)") (PNC) aimed at combatting violent crime and extortions.[[183]](#cite_note-185) The GECE will consist of 400 motorized officers who will patrol different regions of the country in phases. At the request of Arévalo, the United States government donated equipment to support the new task force.[[184]](#cite_note-186)

On 23 April 2024, during a public event marking the first 100 days of his government, Arévalo historically fulfilled one of his campaign promises by reducing the presidential salary by 25%.[[185]](#cite_note-187) As a result of this reduction, the head of state of Guatemala is no longer the highest-paid president in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"). Concurrently, Vice President [Herrera](/wiki/Karin_Herrera "Karin Herrera") also announced a 25% reduction in her salary.[[186]](#cite_note-188)

Arévalo's opposition has sought to weaken his administration through prosecutions of Semilla party members[[175]](#cite_note-:4-177) and indigenous leaders of the 2023 protests that led to his election being upheld.[[187]](#cite_note-189)[[188]](#cite_note-190)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Guatemala](/wiki/Geography_of_Guatemala "Geography of Guatemala")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Guatemala_WV_regions_map_2016_EN.png/250px-Guatemala_WV_regions_map_2016_EN.png)](/wiki/File:Guatemala_WV_regions_map_2016_EN.png)

A map of Guatemala

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Guatemala_K%C3%B6ppen.svg/250px-Guatemala_K%C3%B6ppen.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Guatemala_K%C3%B6ppen.svg)

Köppen climate types of Guatemala

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Quetzaltenango_farm_highlands_2009.jpg/250px-Quetzaltenango_farm_highlands_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quetzaltenango_farm_highlands_2009.jpg)

The highlands of [Quetzaltenango](/wiki/Quetzaltenango "Quetzaltenango")

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Guatemala_town_volcanic_crater.jpg/250px-Guatemala_town_volcanic_crater.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guatemala_town_volcanic_crater.jpg)

A town along the [Pan-American Highway](/wiki/Pan-American_Highway "Pan-American Highway") within a volcanic crater

Guatemala is mountainous with small patches of desert and sand dunes, all hilly valleys, except for the south coast and the vast northern lowlands of [Petén](/wiki/Pet%C3%A9n_(department) "Petén (department)") department. Two mountain chains enter Guatemala from west to east, dividing Guatemala into three major regions: the highlands, where the mountains are located; the Pacific coast, south of the mountains and the Petén region, north of the mountains.

All major cities are located in the highlands and Pacific coast regions; by comparison, Petén is sparsely populated. These three regions vary in climate, elevation, and landscape, providing dramatic contrasts between hot, humid tropical lowlands and colder, drier highland peaks. [Volcán Tajumulco](/wiki/Volc%C3%A1n_Tajumulco "Volcán Tajumulco"), at 4,220 metres (13,850 feet), is the highest point in the Central American countries.

The rivers are short and shallow in the Pacific drainage basin, larger and deeper in the Caribbean and the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico") drainage basins. These rivers include the [Polochic](/wiki/Polochic_River "Polochic River") and [Dulce Rivers](/wiki/Dulce_River_(Guatemala) "Dulce River (Guatemala)"), which drain into [Lake Izabal](/wiki/Lake_Izabal "Lake Izabal"), the [Motagua River](/wiki/Motagua_River "Motagua River"), the [Sarstún](/wiki/Sarstoon_River "Sarstoon River"), which forms the boundary with Belize, and the [Usumacinta River](/wiki/Usumacinta_River "Usumacinta River"), which forms the boundary between Petén and [Chiapas](/wiki/Chiapas "Chiapas"), Mexico.

### Natural disasters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Natural disasters")]

For the 2018 volcanic eruption, see [2018 Volcán de Fuego eruption](/wiki/2018_Volc%C3%A1n_de_Fuego_eruption "2018 Volcán de Fuego eruption").

Guatemala's location between the Caribbean Sea and Pacific Ocean makes it a target for hurricanes such as [Hurricane Mitch](/wiki/Hurricane_Mitch "Hurricane Mitch") in 1998 and [Hurricane Stan](/wiki/Hurricane_Stan "Hurricane Stan") in October 2005, which killed more than 1,500 people. The damage was not wind-related, but rather due to significant [flooding](/wiki/Flooding "Flooding") and resulting [mudslides](/wiki/Mudflow "Mudflow"). The most recent was [Hurricane Eta](/wiki/Hurricane_Eta "Hurricane Eta") in November 2020, which was responsible for more than 100 people missing or killed with the final tally still uncertain.[[189]](#cite_note-191)

Guatemala's highlands lie along the [Motagua Fault](/wiki/Motagua_Fault "Motagua Fault"), part of the boundary between the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean_Plate "Caribbean Plate") and [North American](/wiki/North_American_Plate "North American Plate") [tectonic plates](/wiki/Tectonic_plates "Tectonic plates"). This fault has been responsible for several major earthquakes in historic times, including a 7.5 magnitude tremor on 4 February 1976 which killed more than 25,000 people. In addition, the [Middle America Trench](/wiki/Middle_America_Trench "Middle America Trench"), a major [subduction zone](/wiki/Subduction_zone "Subduction zone"), lies off the Pacific coast. Here, the [Cocos Plate](/wiki/Cocos_Plate "Cocos Plate") is sinking beneath the Caribbean Plate, producing volcanic activity inland of the coast. Guatemala has 37 volcanoes, four of them active: [Pacaya](/wiki/Pacaya "Pacaya"), [Santiaguito](/wiki/Santiaguito "Santiaguito"), [Fuego](/wiki/Volc%C3%A1n_de_Fuego "Volcán de Fuego"), and [Tacaná](/wiki/Volc%C3%A1n_Tacan%C3%A1 "Volcán Tacaná").

Natural disasters have a long history in this geologically active part of the world. For example, two of the three [moves](/wiki/History_of_Antigua_Guatemala "History of Antigua Guatemala") of the capital of Guatemala have been due to volcanic mudflows in 1541 and earthquakes in 1773.

### Biodiversity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Biodiversity")]

Main article: [Biodiversity of Guatemala](/wiki/Biodiversity_of_Guatemala "Biodiversity of Guatemala")

Guatemala has 14 ecoregions ranging from mangrove forests to both ocean littorals with five different ecosystems. Guatemala has 252 listed wetlands, including five lakes, 61 lagoons, 100 rivers, and four swamps.[[190]](#cite_note-192) [Tikal](/wiki/Tikal "Tikal") National Park was the first mixed UNESCO [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"). Guatemala is a country of distinct [fauna](/wiki/Fauna "Fauna"). It has some 1246 known species. Of these, 6.7% are [endemic](/wiki/Endemism "Endemism") and 8.1% are threatened. Guatemala is home to at least 8,682 species of vascular plants, of which 13.5% are endemic. 5.4% of Guatemala is protected under [IUCN](/wiki/International_Union_for_Conservation_of_Nature "International Union for Conservation of Nature") categories I-V.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Maya Biosphere Reserve](/wiki/Maya_Biosphere_Reserve "Maya Biosphere Reserve") in the department of [Petén](/wiki/Pet%C3%A9n_(department) "Petén (department)") has 2,112,940 ha,[[191]](#cite_note-193) making it the second-largest forest in Central America after [Bosawas](/wiki/Bosawas "Bosawas"). Guatemala had a 2019 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 3.85/10, ranking it 138th globally out of 172 countries.[[192]](#cite_note-FLII-Supplementary-194)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

### Political system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Political system")]

Main article: [Politics of Guatemala](/wiki/Politics_of_Guatemala "Politics of Guatemala")

The [National Palace](/wiki/National_Palace_(Guatemala) "National Palace (Guatemala)"), former residence of the president, now used for important acts by the executive branch.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Congreso_de_Guatemala_%287895588940%29.jpg/250px-Congreso_de_Guatemala_%287895588940%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Congreso_de_Guatemala_(7895588940).jpg)

The interior of the [Congress](/wiki/Congress_of_the_Republic_of_Guatemala "Congress of the Republic of Guatemala").

Guatemala is a constitutional democratic republic whereby the [President of Guatemala](/wiki/President_of_Guatemala "President of Guatemala") is both [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") and [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"), and of a [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system"). [Executive power](/wiki/Executive_power "Executive power") is exercised by the government. [Legislative power](/wiki/Legislative_power "Legislative power") is vested in both the government and the [Congress of the Republic](/wiki/Congress_of_the_Republic_of_Guatemala "Congress of the Republic of Guatemala"). The [judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary "Judiciary") is independent of the executive and the legislature.

On 2 September 2015, [Otto Pérez Molina](/wiki/Otto_P%C3%A9rez_Molina "Otto Pérez Molina") resigned as President of Guatemala due to [a corruption scandal](/wiki/La_Linea_corruption_case "La Linea corruption case") and was replaced by [Alejandro Maldonado](/wiki/Alejandro_Maldonado "Alejandro Maldonado") until January 2016.[[193]](#cite_note-Maldonado-195) Congress appointed former [Universidad de San Carlos](/wiki/Universidad_de_San_Carlos_de_Guatemala "Universidad de San Carlos de Guatemala") President Alfonso Fuentes Soria as the new vice president to replace Maldonado.[[194]](#cite_note-soria-196) [Jimmy Morales](/wiki/Jimmy_Morales "Jimmy Morales") subsequently assumed office on 14 January 2016.[[170]](#cite_note-auto1-172) In January 2020, he was succeeded by [Alejandro Giammattei](/wiki/Alejandro_Giammattei "Alejandro Giammattei").[[172]](#cite_note-auto-174)

[César Bernardo Arévalo de León](/wiki/Bernardo_Ar%C3%A9valo "Bernardo Arévalo"),[[195]](#cite_note-congr-197) a Guatemalan diplomat, [sociologist](/wiki/Sociologist "Sociologist"), writer, and politician, and a member and co-founder of the [Semilla](/wiki/Movimiento_Semilla "Movimiento Semilla") party, is now serving as the 52nd [president of Guatemala](/wiki/President_of_Guatemala "President of Guatemala").

Guatemala is divided into 22 [departments](/wiki/Department_(subnational_entity) "Department (subnational entity)") ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *departamentos*) and sub-divided into about 335 municipalities ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *municipios*).[[196]](#cite_note-segeplan-198) Each department has a governor who is appointed by the central government and is assisted through a development council that includes the mayors of the respective department.

### Foreign relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Foreign relations")]

Main article: [Foreign relations of Guatemala](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Guatemala "Foreign relations of Guatemala")

Guatemala has long claimed all or part of the territory of neighboring Belize. Owing to this territorial dispute, Guatemala did not recognize Belize's independence until 6 September 1991,[[197]](#cite_note-199) but the dispute is not resolved. Negotiations are currently under way under the auspices of the [Organization of American States](/wiki/Organization_of_American_States "Organization of American States") to conclude it.[[198]](#cite_note-200)[[199]](#cite_note-201)

### Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Military")]

Main article: [Military of Guatemala](/wiki/Military_of_Guatemala "Military of Guatemala")

Guatemala has a modest military, with between 15,000 and 20,000 personnel.[[200]](#cite_note-202)

In 2017, Guatemala signed the United Nations [Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Prohibition_of_Nuclear_Weapons "Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons").[[201]](#cite_note-203)

### Human rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Human rights")]

See also: [Human rights in Guatemala](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Guatemala "Human rights in Guatemala"), [Violence against women in Guatemala](/wiki/Violence_against_women_in_Guatemala "Violence against women in Guatemala"), and [Guatemalan Civil War](/wiki/Guatemalan_Civil_War "Guatemalan Civil War")

Killings and [death squads](/wiki/Death_squad "Death squad") have been common in Guatemala since the end of the civil war in 1996. They often had ties to Clandestine Security Apparatuses (*Cuerpos Ilegales y Aparatos Clandestinos de Seguridad – CIACS*), organizations of current and former members of the military involved in organized crime. They had significant influence, now somewhat lessened,[[202]](#cite_note-ic-204) but extrajudicial killings continue.[[203]](#cite_note-yale-205) In July 2004, the [Inter-American Court](/wiki/Inter-American_Court "Inter-American Court") condemned the 18 July 1982 massacre of 188 Achi-Maya in Plan de Sanchez, and for the first time in its history, ruled the Guatemalan Army had committed genocide. It was the first ruling by the court against the Guatemalan state for any of the 626 massacres reported in its 1980s scorched-earth campaign.[[203]](#cite_note-yale-205) In those massacres, 83 percent of the victims were Maya and 17 percent Ladino.[[203]](#cite_note-yale-205)

| Extra-Judicial Killings in Guatemala | |
| --- | --- |
| 2010 | 5,072 |
| 2011 | 279 |
| 2012 | 439 |
| source: Center for Legal Action in Human Rights (CALDH)[[202]](#cite_note-ic-204) | |

In 2008, Guatemala became the first country to officially recognize [femicide](/wiki/Femicide "Femicide"), the murder of a female because of her sex, as a crime.[[204]](#cite_note-bbc-206) Guatemala has the third-highest femicide rate in the world, after [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador") and [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica"), with around 9.1 murders for every 100,000 women from 2007 to 2012.[[204]](#cite_note-bbc-206)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Guatemala](/wiki/Economy_of_Guatemala "Economy of Guatemala")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/GDP_per_capita_development_in_El_Salvador%2C_Guatemala_and_Honduras.svg/330px-GDP_per_capita_development_in_El_Salvador%2C_Guatemala_and_Honduras.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GDP_per_capita_development_in_El_Salvador,_Guatemala_and_Honduras.svg)

Historical GDP per capita development of El Salvador, Guatemala and Honduras

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Fields_in_Quetzaltenango.jpg/250px-Fields_in_Quetzaltenango.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fields_in_Quetzaltenango.jpg)

Fields in [Quetzaltenango](/wiki/Quetzaltenango "Quetzaltenango")

Guatemala is the largest economy in Central America, with an estimated 2024 GDP (PPP) per capita of US$10,998. However, Guatemala faces many social problems and is one of the poorest countries in Latin America. The income distribution is highly unequal with more than half of the population below the national poverty line and just over 400,000 (3.2%) unemployed. The CIA World Fact Book considers 56.0% of the population of Guatemala to be living in poverty in 2023.[[205]](#cite_note-cia-207)[[206]](#cite_note-208)

In 2010, the Guatemalan economy grew by 3%, recovering gradually from the 2009 crisis, as a result of the falling demands from the United States and other [Central American](/wiki/Central_American "Central American") markets and the slowdown in foreign investment in the middle of the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession").[[207]](#cite_note-209)

[Remittances](/wiki/Remittance "Remittance") from Guatemalans living in United States now constitute the largest single source of foreign income (two-thirds of exports and one tenth of GDP).[[205]](#cite_note-cia-207)

Some of Guatemala's main exports are fruits, vegetables, flowers, handicrafts, and cloths. It is a leading exporter of [cardamom](/wiki/Cardamom "Cardamom")[[208]](#cite_note-210) and [coffee](/wiki/Coffee "Coffee").[[209]](#cite_note-211)

In the face of a rising demand for [biofuels](/wiki/Biofuel "Biofuel"), the country is growing and exporting an increasing amount of raw materials for biofuel production, especially [sugar cane](/wiki/Sugar_cane "Sugar cane") and [palm oil](/wiki/Palm_oil "Palm oil"). Critics say that this development leads to higher prices for [staple foods](/wiki/Staple_food "Staple food") like corn, a major ingredient in the Guatemalan diet. As a consequence of the subsidization of US American corn, Guatemala imports nearly half of its corn from the United States that is using 40 percent of its crop harvest for biofuel production.[[210]](#cite_note-212) In 2014, the government was considering ways to legalize poppy and marijuana production, hoping to tax production and use tax revenues to fund drug prevention programs and other social projects.[[211]](#cite_note-213)

Gross Domestic Product (GDP) in [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") (PPP) in 2010 was estimated at US$70.15 billion. The service sector is the largest component of GDP at 63%, followed by the industry sector at 23.8% and the agriculture sector at 13.2% (2010 est.). Mines produce gold, silver, zinc, cobalt and nickel.[[212]](#cite_note-214) Country's gold production in 2015 is 6 tons.[[213]](#cite_note-215) The agricultural sector accounts for about two-fifths of exports, and half of the labor force. Organic coffee, sugar, textiles, fresh vegetables, and bananas are the country's main exports. Inflation was 3.9% in 2010.

The 1996 peace accords that ended the decades-long civil war removed a major obstacle to foreign investment. Tourism has become an increasing source of revenue for Guatemala thanks to the new foreign investment.

In March 2006, Guatemala's congress ratified the [Dominican Republic–Central America Free Trade Agreement](/wiki/Dominican_Republic%E2%80%93Central_America_Free_Trade_Agreement "Dominican Republic–Central America Free Trade Agreement") (DR-CAFTA) between several Central American nations and the US.[[214]](#cite_note-216) Guatemala also has [free trade agreements](/wiki/Free_trade_agreement "Free trade agreement") with [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") and [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"). Guatemala was ranked 123rd in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[215]](#cite_note-217)[[216]](#cite_note-218)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Tourism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Santa_Catalina_Arch_-_Antigua_Guatemala_Feb_2020.jpg/250px-Santa_Catalina_Arch_-_Antigua_Guatemala_Feb_2020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Santa_Catalina_Arch_-_Antigua_Guatemala_Feb_2020.jpg)

The colonial city of [Antigua Guatemala](/wiki/Antigua_Guatemala "Antigua Guatemala"), a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

Main article: [Tourism in Guatemala](/wiki/Tourism_in_Guatemala "Tourism in Guatemala")

Tourism has become one of the main drivers of the economy, with tourism estimated at $1.8 billion to the economy in 2008. Guatemala receives around two million tourists annually. In recent years, there has been an increase in the number of cruise ships visiting Guatemalan seaports, leading to higher tourist numbers. Tourist destinations include Maya archaeological sites (e.g. [Tikal](/wiki/Tikal "Tikal") in the Peten, Quiriguá in Izabal, Iximche in Chimaltenango and [Guatemala City](/wiki/Guatemala_City "Guatemala City")), natural attractions (e.g. [Lake Atitlán](/wiki/Lake_Atitl%C3%A1n "Lake Atitlán") and [Semuc Champey](/wiki/Semuc_Champey "Semuc Champey")) and historical sites such as the colonial city of [Antigua Guatemala](/wiki/Antigua_Guatemala "Antigua Guatemala"), which is recognized as a UNESCO Cultural Heritage site.

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Main articles: [Demographics of Guatemala](/wiki/Demographics_of_Guatemala "Demographics of Guatemala") and [List of cities in Guatemala](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Guatemala "List of cities in Guatemala")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Santiago_de_atitlan_natives_2009.JPG/250px-Santiago_de_atitlan_natives_2009.JPG)](/wiki/File:Santiago_de_atitlan_natives_2009.JPG)

[Tz'utujil](/wiki/Tz%27utujil_people "Tz'utujil people") men in [Santiago Atitlán](/wiki/Santiago_Atitl%C3%A1n "Santiago Atitlán")

Guatemala has a population of 17,608,483 (2021 est).[[13]](#cite_note-UN_WPP-15)[[14]](#cite_note-UN_WPP_2022-16) With only 885,000 in 1900, this constitutes the fastest population growth in the Western Hemisphere during the 20th century.[[217]](#cite_note-219) The Republic of Guatemala's first census was taken in 1778.[[218]](#cite_note-:0-220) The census records for 1778, 1880, 1893 and 1921 were used as scrap paper and no longer exist, although their statistical information was preserved.[[219]](#cite_note-:3-221) Censuses have not been taken at regular intervals. The 1837 census was discredited at the time; statistician Don Jose de la Valle made a calculation that in 1837 the population of Guatemala was 600,000.[[218]](#cite_note-:0-220) The 1940 census was burned.[[220]](#cite_note-:1-222)[[219]](#cite_note-:3-221)

### Ethnic groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Ethnic groups")]

Main article: [Demographics of Guatemala § Ethnic groups](/wiki/Demographics_of_Guatemala#Ethnic_groups "Demographics of Guatemala")

Racial groups in Guatemala (2018 census)[[2]](#cite_note-censopoblacion.gt-2)

1. [Ladinos](/wiki/Ladino_people "Ladino people") (56.0%)
2. [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Guatemala "Indigenous peoples in Guatemala") (43.4%)
3. [Blacks](/wiki/Black_Guatemalans "Black Guatemalans") (0.32%)
4. Others (0.24%)

Guatemala is populated by a variety of ethnic, cultural, racial, and linguistic groups. According to the 2018 Census conducted by the [National Institute of Statistics (INE)](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Statistics_(Guatemala) "National Institute of Statistics (Guatemala)"), 56% of the population is [Ladino](/wiki/Ladino_people "Ladino people") reflecting mixed indigenous and European heritage.[[222]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-224) Indigenous Guatemalans are 43.6% of the national population, which is one of the largest percentages in the Americas, behind only Peru and Bolivia. Most indigenous Guatemalans (41.7% of the national population) are of the [Maya people](/wiki/Maya_people "Maya people"), namely [K'iche'](/wiki/K%27iche%27_people "K'iche' people") (11.0% of the total population), [Q'eqchi](/wiki/Q%27eqchi%27_people "Q'eqchi' people") (8.3%), [Kaqchikel](/wiki/Kaqchikel_people "Kaqchikel people") (7.8%), [Mam](/wiki/Mam_people "Mam people") (5.2%), and "other [Maya](/wiki/Maya_peoples "Maya peoples")" (7.6%). 2% of the national population is indigenous non-Maya. 1.8% of the population is Xinca (mesoamerican), and 0.1% of the population is Garifuna (African/Carib mix).[[222]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-224) "However, indigenous rights activists put the indigenous figure closer to 61 per cent."
[[223]](#cite_note-225)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Guatemala_ethnie_maya_2018.png/250px-Guatemala_ethnie_maya_2018.png)](/wiki/File:Guatemala_ethnie_maya_2018.png)

Percentage of people who identify as Mayan, by municipality (2018)

[White](/wiki/White_people "White people") Guatemalans of European descent, also called [Criollo](/wiki/Criollo_people "Criollo people"), are not differentiated from Ladinos (mixed-race) individuals in the Guatemalan census.[[222]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-224) Most are descendants of German and Spanish settlers, and others derive from Italians, British, French, Swiss, Belgians, Dutch, Russians and Danes. German settlers are credited with bringing the tradition of Christmas trees to Guatemala.[[224]](#cite_note-226)

The population includes about 110,000 Salvadorans.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [Garífuna](/wiki/Gar%C3%ADfuna "Garífuna"), descended primarily from Black Africans who lived and intermarried with indigenous peoples from St. Vincent, live mainly in [Livingston](/wiki/Livingston_(Guatemala) "Livingston (Guatemala)") and [Puerto Barrios](/wiki/Puerto_Barrios "Puerto Barrios"). [Afro-Guatemalans](/wiki/Afro-Guatemalan "Afro-Guatemalan") and [mulattos](/wiki/Mulatto "Mulatto") are descended primarily from banana plantation workers. There are also [Asians](/wiki/Asian_people "Asian people"), mostly of [Chinese](/wiki/Han_Chinese "Han Chinese") descent but also mostly Christian [Arabs](/wiki/Arab_people "Arab people") of [Lebanese](/wiki/Lebanese_people "Lebanese people"), [Palestinian](/wiki/Palestinian "Palestinian") and [Syrian](/wiki/Syrian_people "Syrian people") descent.[[225]](#cite_note-227)

### Languages

Main articles: [Languages of Guatemala](/wiki/Languages_of_Guatemala "Languages of Guatemala") and [Guatemalan Spanish](/wiki/Guatemalan_Spanish "Guatemalan Spanish")

Languages in Guatemala (native speakers)[[226]](#cite_note-228)

Guatemala's sole official language is Spanish. It is spoken by 93% of the population[[227]](#cite_note-229) and is estimated to be the mother tongue of 75.9% of the population.

Twenty-one [Mayan languages](/wiki/Mayan_languages "Mayan languages") are spoken, especially in rural areas, as well as two non-Mayan [Indigenous](/wiki/Amerindian "Amerindian") languages: [Xinca](/wiki/Xinca_language "Xinca language"), which is indigenous to the country, and [Garifuna](/wiki/Garifuna_language "Garifuna language"), an [Arawakan](/wiki/Arawakan "Arawakan") language spoken on the Caribbean coast. According to the Language Law of 2003, these languages are recognized as national languages.[[228]](#cite_note-Decreto19-2003-230)

### Indigenous integration and bilingual education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Indigenous integration and bilingual education")]

Main article: [Intercultural bilingual education in Guatemala](/wiki/Intercultural_bilingual_education_in_Guatemala "Intercultural bilingual education in Guatemala")

Throughout the 20th century there have been many developments in the integration of [Mayan languages](/wiki/Mayan_languages "Mayan languages") into the Guatemalan society and educational system. Originating from political reasons, these processes have aided the revival of some Mayan languages and advanced bilingual education in the country.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Idiomasmap_Guatemala.svg/250px-Idiomasmap_Guatemala.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Idiomasmap_Guatemala.svg)

Language map of Guatemala. The "Castilian" areas represent Spanish.

In 1945, in order to overcome "the Indian problem", the Guatemalan government founded The Institute Indigents ta National (NH), the purpose of which was to teach literacy to Mayan children in their mother tongue instead of Spanish, to prepare the ground for later assimilation of the latter. The teaching of literacy in the first language, which received support from the UN, significantly advanced in 1952, when the SIL (Summer Institute of Linguistics), located in Dallas, Texas, partnered with the Guatemalan Ministry of Education; within 2 years, numerous written works in Mayan languages had been printed and published, and vast advancement was done in the translation of the New Testament. Further efforts to integrate the indigenous into the Ladino[[229]](#cite_note-231) society were made in the following years, including the invention of a special alphabet to assist Mayan students transition to Spanish, and bilingual education in the Q'eqchi' area. When Spanish became the official language of Guatemala in 1965, the government started several programs, such as the Bilingual [Castellanizacion](/wiki/Castellanizaci%C3%B3n "Castellanización") Program and the Radiophonic Schools, to accelerate the move of Mayan students to Spanish. Unintentionally, the efforts to integrate the indigenous population using language, especially the new alphabet, gave institutions tools to use Mayan languages in schools, and while improving Mayan children's learning, they left them unequipped to learn in a solely Spanish environment. So, an additional expansion of bilingual education took place in 1980, when an experimental program in which children were to be instructed in their mother tongue until they are fluent enough in Spanish was created. The program proved successful when the students of the pilot showed higher academic achievements than the ones in the Spanish-only control schools. In 1987, when the pilot was to finish, bilingual education was made official in Guatemala.

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Religion")]

Main article: [Religion in Guatemala](/wiki/Religion_in_Guatemala "Religion in Guatemala")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Guatemala_City_3._catedral.jpg/250px-Guatemala_City_3._catedral.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guatemala_City_3._catedral.jpg)

The [Catedral Metropolitana](/wiki/Cathedral_of_Guatemala_City "Cathedral of Guatemala City"), Guatemala City

[Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") is very influential in nearly all of [Guatemalan society](/wiki/Demographics_of_Guatemala "Demographics of Guatemala"), both in cosmology and social-politic composition. The country, once dominated by the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") (introduced by the Spanish during the colonial era), is now influenced by a diversity of Christian denominations. Between 2001 and 2012, the Catholic Church declined from 55% to 47.9% of people.[[230]](#cite_note-232) During this time, the already numerous [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") population grew from 30% to 38.2%. Those claiming no religious affiliation were down from 12.7% to 11.6%. The remainder, including [Mormons](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints_in_Guatemala "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints in Guatemala"), [Jews](/wiki/Jews_in_Guatemala "Jews in Guatemala"), [Muslims](/wiki/Islam_in_Guatemala "Islam in Guatemala"), and [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), remained at more than 2% of the population.[[231]](#cite_note-233)

Since the 1960s, and particularly since the end of the civil war in the 1980s, Guatemala has experienced rapid growth in Protestantism, especially [Evangelical](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism") and [Pentecostal](/wiki/Pentecostal "Pentecostal") varieties, owing to heightened missionary activity. Protestant Christianity is now the largest religious denomination in Guatemala, at 45.7% of the population.[[205]](#cite_note-cia-207) Guatemala has been described as the most heavily evangelical nation in Latin America,[[232]](#cite_note-234) with multitudes of unregistered churches,[[233]](#cite_note-235) matched or exceeded only by Brazil or [Honduras](/wiki/Protestantism_in_Honduras "Protestantism in Honduras").[[234]](#cite_note-236) Growth is particularly strong among the ethnic Maya population, with the [National Evangelical Presbyterian Church of Guatemala](/wiki/National_Evangelical_Presbyterian_Church_of_Guatemala "National Evangelical Presbyterian Church of Guatemala") maintaining 11 indigenous-language presbyteries. [The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") grew from 40,000 members in 1984 to 164,000 in 1998 and continues to expand.[[235]](#cite_note-237)[[236]](#cite_note-238)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/San_andres_xecul_church_2009.JPG/250px-San_andres_xecul_church_2009.JPG)](/wiki/File:San_andres_xecul_church_2009.JPG)

A colonial church in [San Andrés Xecul](/wiki/San_Andr%C3%A9s_Xecul "San Andrés Xecul")

The growth of [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") in Guatemala has been especially strong, with hundreds of thousands of converts in the last five years,[[237]](#cite_note-EOC3-239)[[238]](#cite_note-240)[[239]](#cite_note-241)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] giving the country the highest proportion of Orthodox adherents in the Western Hemisphere.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Traditional [Maya religion](/wiki/Maya_religion "Maya religion") persists through the process of [inculturation](/wiki/Inculturation "Inculturation"), in which certain practices are incorporated into Catholic ceremonies and worship when they are sympathetic to the meaning of Catholic belief.[[240]](#cite_note-242)[[241]](#cite_note-243) Indigenous religious practices are increasing as a result of the cultural protections established under the peace accords.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The government has instituted a policy of providing altars at every Maya ruin to facilitate traditional ceremonies.

### Immigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Immigration")]

Main article: [Immigration to Guatemala](/wiki/Immigration_to_Guatemala "Immigration to Guatemala")

During the colonial era Guatemala received immigrants (settlers) only from Spain. In the mid 19th century and early 20th century Guatemala received waves of immigration from Europe, [*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] primarily from Germany. These immigrants installed coffee and cardamom [fincas](/wiki/Fincas "Fincas") in [Alta Verapaz](/wiki/Alta_Verapaz "Alta Verapaz"), [Zacapa](/wiki/Zacapa "Zacapa"), [Quetzaltenango](/wiki/Quetzaltenango "Quetzaltenango"), [Baja Verapaz](/wiki/Baja_Verapaz "Baja Verapaz") and [Izabal](/wiki/Izabal_Department "Izabal Department"). To a lesser extent people came from Spain, France, Belgium, England, Italy, Sweden, and other European countries.

Many European immigrants to Guatemala were politicians, refugees, and entrepreneurs as well as families looking to settle. Up to 1950 Guatemala was the Central American country that received the most immigrants, behind [Costa Rica](/wiki/Costa_Rica "Costa Rica"), and large numbers of immigrants are still received today.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Since the 1890s, there has been immigration from East Asia.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Also, beginning with the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"), the immigrant population is being strengthened by [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish "Jewish") immigration.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During the second half of the twentieth century, Latin American immigration to Guatemala increased, particularly from Mexico, Cuba, and Argentina, although most of these immigrants stayed only temporarily before proceeding to their final destinations in the United States.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Currently, the largest groups of immigrants in Guatemala come from neighboring El Salvador and Mexico.

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in Guatemala](/wiki/Education_in_Guatemala "Education in Guatemala")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Recursos_Educativos.jpg/250px-Recursos_Educativos.jpg)](/wiki/File:Recursos_Educativos.jpg)

Library of the [University of San Carlos](/wiki/Universidad_de_San_Carlos_de_Guatemala "Universidad de San Carlos de Guatemala"), fourth university founded in the Americas.

Education in Guatemala is for the most part provided publicly, funded, and overseen by the central government. The [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_(Guatemala) "Ministry of Education (Guatemala)") is responsible for the formulation, implementation, and supervision of national educational policy and curriculum. Education is divided into a five-tier system which includes [primary education](/wiki/Primary_education "Primary education"), followed by [secondary education](/wiki/Secondary_school "Secondary school"), and [tertiary education](/wiki/Tertiary_education "Tertiary education"), depending on the level of technical training. Education is instructed in Spanish, though a bilingual education in [Amerindian languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Guatemala "Languages of Guatemala") is available in regions with predominantly indigenous populations.[[243]](#cite_note-245) Guatemala has a total of [fifteen universities](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_Guatemala "List of universities in Guatemala"); one public and fourteen private. Established in 1676, [Universidad de San Carlos](/wiki/Universidad_de_San_Carlos_de_Guatemala "Universidad de San Carlos de Guatemala") is the oldest post-secondary institution in Guatemala and the fourth oldest in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas").[[244]](#cite_note-246)

Guatemala spends about 3.2 percent of its GDP on education.[[245]](#cite_note-247) However, [youth participation](/wiki/Youth_in_Guatemala "Youth in Guatemala") has been an ongoing challenge—particularly in rural areas and indigenous communities.[[246]](#cite_note-248) Lack of training for rural teachers is one of the key contributors to Guatemala's low literacy rates. Nevertheless, significant strides in education have made literacy rates among the population aged 15 and above increase from 74.5% in 2012 to 82.1% in 2024.[[247]](#cite_note-249)[[205]](#cite_note-cia-207) Organizations such as [Child Aid](/wiki/Child_Aid "Child Aid"), [Pueblo a Pueblo](/wiki/Pueblo_a_Pueblo "Pueblo a Pueblo"), and [Common Hope](/wiki/Common_Hope "Common Hope"), which train teachers in communities throughout the Central Highlands region, have also worked to improve educational outcomes for children.

### Health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Health")]

Main article: [Health in Guatemala](/wiki/Health_in_Guatemala "Health in Guatemala")

Guatemala has among the worst health outcomes in Latin America with some of the highest infant mortality rates, and one of the lowest life expectancies at birth in the region.[[248]](#cite_note-250) With about 16,000 doctors for its 16 million people, Guatemala has about half the doctor-citizen ratio recommended by the [WHO](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization").[[249]](#cite_note-251) Since the end of the [Guatemalan Civil War](/wiki/Guatemalan_Civil_War "Guatemalan Civil War") in 1997, the Ministry of Health has extended healthcare access to 54% of the rural population.[[250]](#cite_note-www-wds.worldbank.org-252)

Healthcare has received different levels of support from different political administrations who disagree on how best to manage distribution of services – via a private or a public entity – and the scale of financing that should be made available.[[250]](#cite_note-www-wds.worldbank.org-252) As of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit), the Ministry of Health lacked the financial means to monitor or evaluate its programs.[[250]](#cite_note-www-wds.worldbank.org-252)

Total healthcare spending, both public and private, has remained constant at between 6.4 and 7.3% of the GDP.[[251]](#cite_note-253)[[252]](#cite_note-who.int-254) Per-capita average annual healthcare spending was only $368 in 2012.[[252]](#cite_note-who.int-254) Guatemalan patients choose between indigenous treatments or Western medicine when they engage with the health system.[[253]](#cite_note-255)

In the 2024 Global Hunger Index (GHI), Guatemala ranks 81st out of 127 countries with sufficient data. Guatemala's GHI score is 18.8, which indicates a moderate level of hunger.[[254]](#cite_note-256)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Culture")]

Main article: [Culture of Guatemala](/wiki/Culture_of_Guatemala "Culture of Guatemala")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Baile_de_la_Conquista.jpg/250px-Baile_de_la_Conquista.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baile_de_la_Conquista.jpg)

Traditional [dance of the conquest](/wiki/Baile_de_la_Conquista "Baile de la Conquista")

Guatemala City is home to many of the nation's libraries and museums, including the National Archives, the National Library, and the Museum of Archeology and Ethnology, which has an extensive collection of Maya artifacts. It also boasts private museums such as the [Ixchel Museum of Indigenous Textiles and Clothing](/wiki/Ixchel_Museum_of_Indigenous_Textiles_and_Clothing "Ixchel Museum of Indigenous Textiles and Clothing") and the [Museo Popol Vuh](/wiki/Museo_Popol_Vuh "Museo Popol Vuh"), which focuses on Maya archaeology. Both these museums are housed on the [Universidad Francisco Marroquín](/wiki/Universidad_Francisco_Marroqu%C3%ADn "Universidad Francisco Marroquín") campus. Most of the 329 municipalities in the country have at least a small museum.

### Art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Art")]

Guatemala has produced many indigenous artists who follow centuries-old Pre-Columbian traditions. Reflecting Guatemala's colonial and post-colonial history, encounters with multiple global art movements also have produced a wealth of artists who have combined the traditional [primitivist](/wiki/Primitivism "Primitivism") or [naive aesthetic](/wiki/Na%C3%AFve_art "Naïve art") with European, North American, and other traditions.

The [Escuela Nacional de Artes Plásticas "Rafael Rodríguez Padilla"](/wiki/Escuela_Nacional_de_Artes_Pl%C3%A1sticas_%22Rafael_Rodr%C3%ADguez_Padilla%22 "Escuela Nacional de Artes Plásticas \"Rafael Rodríguez Padilla\"") is Guatemala's leading art school, and several leading indigenous artists, also graduates of that school, have work in the permanent collection of the [Museo Nacional de Arte Moderno](/wiki/Museo_Nacional_de_Arte_Moderno_%22Carlos_M%C3%A9rida%22 "Museo Nacional de Arte Moderno \"Carlos Mérida\"") in the capital city. Contemporary Guatemalan artists who have gained reputations outside of Guatemala include Dagoberto Vásquez, [Luis Rolando Ixquiac Xicara](/wiki/Luis_Rolando_Ixquiac_Xicara "Luis Rolando Ixquiac Xicara"), [Carlos Mérida](/wiki/Carlos_M%C3%A9rida "Carlos Mérida"),[[255]](#cite_note-257) [Aníbal López](/wiki/An%C3%ADbal_L%C3%B3pez "Aníbal López"), [Roberto González Goyri](/wiki/Roberto_Gonz%C3%A1lez_Goyri "Roberto González Goyri"), and [Elmar René Rojas](/wiki/Elmar_Rojas "Elmar Rojas").[[256]](#cite_note-258)

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Literature")]

Further information: [Guatemalan literature](/wiki/Guatemalan_literature "Guatemalan literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/20240620_NP_PRESIDENTE_BERNARDO_AREVALO_Y_KARIN_HERRERA_PARTICIPAN_EN_DIALOGO_PREMIO_NOBEL_DE_LA_PAZ_054_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-20240620_NP_PRESIDENTE_BERNARDO_AREVALO_Y_KARIN_HERRERA_PARTICIPAN_EN_DIALOGO_PREMIO_NOBEL_DE_LA_PAZ_054_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:20240620_NP_PRESIDENTE_BERNARDO_AREVALO_Y_KARIN_HERRERA_PARTICIPAN_EN_DIALOGO_PREMIO_NOBEL_DE_LA_PAZ_054_(cropped).jpg)

Human rights activist, author, and [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") laureate, [Rigoberta Menchú](/wiki/Rigoberta_Mench%C3%BA "Rigoberta Menchú").

* The [Guatemala National Prize in Literature](/wiki/Guatemala_National_Prize_in_Literature "Guatemala National Prize in Literature") is a one-time-only award that recognizes an individual writer's body of work. It has been given annually since 1988 by the Ministry of Culture and Sports.
* [Miguel Ángel Asturias](/wiki/Miguel_%C3%81ngel_Asturias "Miguel Ángel Asturias") won the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") in 1967. Among his famous books is *[El Señor Presidente](/wiki/El_Se%C3%B1or_Presidente "El Señor Presidente")*, a novel based on the government of [Manuel Estrada Cabrera](/wiki/Manuel_Estrada_Cabrera "Manuel Estrada Cabrera").
* [Rigoberta Menchú](/wiki/Rigoberta_Mench%C3%BA "Rigoberta Menchú"), winner of the [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") for fighting oppression of indigenous people in Guatemala, is famous for her books *I, Rigoberta Menchú*[[257]](#cite_note-259) and *Crossing Borders*.[[258]](#cite_note-260)

### Cinema

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Cinema")]

The Guatemalan director [Jayro Bustamante](/wiki/Jayro_Bustamante "Jayro Bustamante") has gained an international audience with his films focused on Guatemalan contemporary society and politics : *Ixcanul* in 2015, and *Temblores* and *La Llorona* (The Weeping Woman) in 2019.

### Media and news

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Media and news")]

Further information: [Media of Guatemala](/wiki/Media_of_Guatemala "Media of Guatemala")

Major national newspapers in Guatemala include *[Prensa Libre](/wiki/Prensa_Libre_(Guatemala) "Prensa Libre (Guatemala)")*, *[El Periodico](/wiki/El_Peri%C3%B3dico_(Guatemala) "El Periódico (Guatemala)")* and *[Siglo21](/wiki/Siglo_Veintiuno "Siglo Veintiuno")*.[[259]](#cite_note-261)[[260]](#cite_note-262) Guatemala also has a few major local channels and radio stations, such as one of Guatemala's major radio stations, *Emisoras Unidas*.

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Music")]

Further information: [Music of Guatemala](/wiki/Music_of_Guatemala "Music of Guatemala")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Concierto_Ricardo_Arjona_Miami_2009.jpg/250px-Concierto_Ricardo_Arjona_Miami_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Concierto_Ricardo_Arjona_Miami_2009.jpg)

[Ricardo Arjona](/wiki/Ricardo_Arjona "Ricardo Arjona"), a singer with a long career, his music is well known in Latin America.

Guatemalan music comprises a number of styles and expressions. Guatemalan social change has been empowered by music such as [nueva cancion](/wiki/Nueva_cancion "Nueva cancion"), which blends together histories, present-day issues, and the political values and struggles of common people. The [Maya](/wiki/Maya_civilization "Maya civilization") had an intense musical practice, as documented by their [iconography](/wiki/Iconography "Iconography").[[261]](#cite_note-263)[[262]](#cite_note-264) Guatemala was also one of the first regions in the New World to be introduced to European music, from 1524 on. Many composers from the Renaissance, baroque, classical, romantic, and contemporary music styles have contributed works of all genres. The [marimba](/wiki/Marimba "Marimba"), which is like a wooden xylophone,[[263]](#cite_note-265) is the national instrument and its music is widely found in Guatemala.[[264]](#cite_note-266) It has developed a large repertoire of very attractive pieces that have been popular for more than a century.

The *Historia General de Guatemala* has published a series of CDs compiling the historical music of Guatemala, in which every style is represented, from the Maya, colonial, independent and republican eras to the present. Many contemporary music groups in Guatemala play [Caribbean music](/wiki/Caribbean_music "Caribbean music"), [salsa](/wiki/Salsa_music "Salsa music"), [Garifuna](/wiki/Garifuna_music "Garifuna music")-influenced [punta](/wiki/Punta "Punta"), [Latin pop](/wiki/Latin_pop "Latin pop"), [Mexican regional](/wiki/Music_of_Mexico "Music of Mexico"), and [mariachi](/wiki/Mariachi "Mariachi").

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

Main article: [Guatemalan cuisine](/wiki/Guatemalan_cuisine "Guatemalan cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/091225_tamales.JPG/250px-091225_tamales.JPG)](/wiki/File:091225_tamales.JPG)

Black and red tamales in Guatemala

Many traditional foods in Guatemalan cuisine are based on Mayan cuisine and prominently feature [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize"), [chilies](/wiki/Chili_pepper "Chili pepper") and [black beans](/wiki/Black_turtle_bean "Black turtle bean") as key ingredients. Traditional dishes also include a variety of stews including [Kak'ik](/wiki/Kak%27ik "Kak'ik") *(Kak-ik)*, which is a tomato-based stew with [turkey](/wiki/Turkey_(bird) "Turkey (bird)"), [pepian](/wiki/Pepi%C3%A1n "Pepián"), and [cocido](/wiki/Cocido "Cocido"). Guatemala is also known for its *[antojitos](/wiki/Mexican_street_food "Mexican street food")*, which include small [tamales](/wiki/Tamale "Tamale") called "*chuchitos*", fried [plantains](/wiki/Cooking_banana "Cooking banana"), and [tostadas](/wiki/Tostada_(tortilla) "Tostada (tortilla)") with [tomato sauce](/wiki/Tomato_sauce "Tomato sauce"), [guacamole](/wiki/Guacamole "Guacamole") or black beans. Certain foods are also commonly eaten on certain days of the week; for example, a popular custom is to eat *paches* (a kind of tamale made from potatoes) on Thursday. Certain dishes are also associated with special occasions, such as [fiambre](/wiki/Fiambre "Fiambre") for [All Saints' Day](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day") on 1 November, or tamales and *ponche* ([fruit punch](/wiki/Punch_(drink) "Punch (drink)")), which are both very common around Christmas.

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guatemala&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Estadio_Mateo_Flores_Guatemala.JPG/250px-Estadio_Mateo_Flores_Guatemala.JPG)](/wiki/File:Estadio_Mateo_Flores_Guatemala.JPG)

[Estadio Doroteo Guamuch Flores](/wiki/Estadio_Doroteo_Guamuch_Flores "Estadio Doroteo Guamuch Flores") in [Guatemala City](/wiki/Guatemala_City "Guatemala City")

Further information: [Football in Guatemala](/wiki/Football_in_Guatemala "Football in Guatemala"), [Guatemala at the Olympics](/wiki/Guatemala_at_the_Olympics "Guatemala at the Olympics"), and [Guatemala at the Paralympics](/wiki/Guatemala_at_the_Paralympics "Guatemala at the Paralympics")

[Football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") is the most popular sport in Guatemala and its [national team](/wiki/Guatemala_national_football_team "Guatemala national football team") has appeared in 18 editions of the [CONCACAF Championship](/wiki/CONCACAF_Championship "CONCACAF Championship"), winning it once, in [1967](/wiki/1967_CONCACAF_Championship "1967 CONCACAF Championship"). However, the team has yet to qualify for the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup"). Established in 1919, the [National Football Federation of Guatemala](/wiki/National_Football_Federation_of_Guatemala "National Football Federation of Guatemala") organizes the country's [national league](/wiki/Liga_Nacional_de_F%C3%BAtbol_de_Guatemala "Liga Nacional de Fútbol de Guatemala") and its lower-level competitions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Futsal](/wiki/Futsal "Futsal") is the most successful [team sport](/wiki/Team_sport "Team sport") in Guatemala. Its [national team](/wiki/Guatemala_national_futsal_team "Guatemala national futsal team") won the [2008 CONCACAF Futsal Championship](/wiki/2008_CONCACAF_Futsal_Championship "2008 CONCACAF Futsal Championship") as hosts. It was also the runner-up in [2012](/wiki/2012_CONCACAF_Futsal_Championship "2012 CONCACAF Futsal Championship") as hosts and won the bronze medal in [2016](/wiki/2016_CONCACAF_Futsal_Championship "2016 CONCACAF Futsal Championship") and [2024](/wiki/2024_CONCACAF_Futsal_Championship "2024 CONCACAF Futsal Championship").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Guatemala participated for the first time in the [FIFA Futsal World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_Futsal_World_Cup "FIFA Futsal World Cup") in [2000](/wiki/2000_FIFA_Futsal_World_Cup "2000 FIFA Futsal World Cup"), as hosts, and has played in every competition from [2008](/wiki/2008_FIFA_Futsal_World_Cup "2008 FIFA Futsal World Cup") onwards. It has never passed the first round. It has also participated in every [Grand Prix de Futsal](/wiki/Grand_Prix_de_Futsal "Grand Prix de Futsal") since [2009](/wiki/2009_Grand_Prix_de_Futsal "2009 Grand Prix de Futsal"), reaching the semifinals in [2014](/wiki/2014_Grand_Prix_de_Futsal "2014 Grand Prix de Futsal").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Guatemalan Olympic Committee](/wiki/Guatemalan_Olympic_Committee "Guatemalan Olympic Committee") was founded in 1947 and recognized by the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") that same year. Guatemala participated in the [1952 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Guatemala_at_the_1952_Summer_Olympics "Guatemala at the 1952 Summer Olympics"), and in every edition since the [1968 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Guatemala_at_the_1968_Summer_Olympics "Guatemala at the 1968 Summer Olympics"). It has also appeared in a single [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympics "Winter Olympics") edition, in [1988](/wiki/Guatemala_at_the_1988_Winter_Olympics "Guatemala at the 1988 Winter Olympics").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
